questions.specification:
A student took three quizzes.
Her mean score was $7$.
Her median score was $6$.
The range of her three scores was $8$ marks.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses mean and median conditions (and range via highest-lowest relationship) to determine the original three quiz scores, i.e., data values are unknown but constrained by measures of central tendency.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 1: Given some values for data, finding each: mean, mode, median and range
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Given some values for data, finding each: mean, mode, median and range
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) uses the mean of four scores to find the missing fourth value, applying the mean relationship to a small data set.

parts.content: Find the number of marks she scored in each of the three quizzes.
parts.markscheme:
<marks> M1 </marks>: For correctly setting up equations based on the given information. For example, letting the scores be $x, y, z$ in ascending order, deduce $y=6$. Set up mean equation: $\frac{x+y+z}{3} = 7$ and range equation: $z-x=8$.
    
<marks> M1 </marks>: For solving the simultaneous equations (e.g., $x+6+z = 21 \implies x+z=15$, and $z-x=8$).
   
<marks> A1 </marks>: Correct scores are $3.5, 6, 11.5$.

parts.content: The student took a fourth quiz. The mean of her four scores was $9$. Find her score in the fourth quiz.
parts.markscheme:
<marks> M1 </marks>: For using the new mean to find the total sum of four scores (e.g., $9 \times 4 = 36$).
    
<marks> M1 </marks>: For subtracting the sum of the first three scores from the new total sum (e.g., $36 - (3.5+6+11.5)$ or $36 - 21$).
    
<marks> A1 </marks>: The score for the fourth quiz is $15$.

questions.specification:
The heights (in cm) of 50 plants are recorded in the following frequency table:

| Height $(\mathrm{cm})$ | Frequency |
| :--- | :---: |
| $20 \leq h<30$ | 10 |
| $30 \leq h<40$ | 15 |
| $40 \leq h<50$ | 12 |
| $50 \leq h<60$ | 8 |
| $60 \leq h<70$ | 5 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 3: Given a frequency table, calculating the mean, median, modal class and range of the data
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Given a frequency table, calculating the mean, median, modal class and range of the data
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses a grouped frequency table to (a) estimate the mean via class midpoints and frequencies, and (b) identify the modal class (highest frequency).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.5—Probability concepts, expected numbers > Question Type 3: Using simple experiments to calculate probabilities
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Using simple experiments to calculate probabilities
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for an estimated probability from frequency data (relative frequency of plants with height ≥ 50 cm).

parts.content: Estimate the mean height of the plants.
parts.markscheme:
<marks>M1</marks>: Midpoints: $25, 35, 45, 55, 65$

<marks>M1</marks>: Mean = $\frac{(25 \times 10) + (35 \times 15) + (45 \times 12) + (55 \times 8) + (65 \times 5)}{50}$ 

<marks>A1</marks>: Mean $= \frac{2080}{50} = 41.6\ \mathrm{cm}$

parts.content: Determine the modal class.
parts.markscheme: <marks>A1</marks>: Modal class: $30 \leq h<40$

parts.content: Estimate the probability that a randomly selected plant has a height of at least 50 cm.

questions.specification:
The scores, $x$, obtained by $15$ students in a Mathematics quiz are shown below.
$32, 45, 28, 39, 41, 35, 25, 48, 30, 42, 38, 36, 40, 33, 44$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 5: Given a data set, finding the value of the standard deviation, variance and IQR
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 5: Given a data set, finding the value of the standard deviation, variance and IQR
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The task is to compute median, lower and upper quartiles, and interquartile range from a raw (ungrouped) data set—explicitly covered by the leaf topic on finding IQR (and related summary statistics) from a data set.

parts.content: Find the median score.
parts.markscheme:
Sort the data in ascending order:
    
<marks> M1 </marks>: $25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36, 38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48$. Number of data values ($N$) $= 15$.
    
Position of median $= \frac{N+1}{2} = \frac{15+1}{2} = 8^{\text{th}}$ value.
    
<marks> A1 </marks>: Median ($Q_2$) $= 38$.

steps.title: Strategy: Manual Sorting
steps.content: To find the median using the **Manual Sorting Approach**, we must first arrange the raw data values in ascending order (from smallest to largest) and then identify the specific value that sits in the middle position.

steps.title: Sort the data
steps.content:
First, we list the scores given in the question and reorder them from lowest to highest.

Raw data:
$$32, 45, 28, 39, 41, 35, 25, 48, 30, 42, 38, 36, 40, 33, 44$$

Sorted data:
$$25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36, 38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48$$

steps.title: Calculate the median position
steps.content:
The dataset contains $N=15$ values. We calculate the rank (position) of the median using the formula $\frac{N+1}{2}$:

$$\text{Position} = \frac{15+1}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8$$

This tells us the median is the **8th** value in the sorted list.

calculator.description: Calculate the position of the median

steps.title: Identify the median value
steps.content:
Now we count to the 8th term in our sorted sequence:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{1st to 7th:} & \quad 25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36 \\ \textbf{8th:} & \quad \mathbf{38} \\ \text{9th to 15th:} & \quad 39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48 \end{aligned}$$

The median score is **38**.

steps.title: Identify the topic
steps.content: This question asks us to find the median of a set of raw data scores. This concept is covered in **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**, specifically section **SL 4.3** (Mean, median, mode). Since we are using the Graphing Calculator (GDC) approach, we will let the technology handle the sorting and calculation.

steps.title: Enter data into calculator
steps.content: Enter the 15 scores into a list on your graphing calculator. You do not need to sort the data yourself; the calculator will do this automatically.

steps.content: Use the statistics calculation function to generate summary statistics for your list. This function computes values like the mean, standard deviation, and the five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max).

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [1:1-Var Stats]. Ensure 'List' is set to L1, then press Calculate.

desmos.instructions: Type 'median(L)' to find the median directly.

steps.title: Read the median value
steps.content:
Scroll down the results screen on your calculator until you see the value labeled **Med**, **Median**, or **Med(x)**. 

The calculator output will display:

$$ \text{Median} = 38 $$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The median score is 38.

parts.content: Find the lower quartile and the upper quartile of the scores.
parts.markscheme:
<marks> M1 </marks>: Lower half of data (values below the median): $25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36$. 

<marks> A1 </marks>: Position of lower quartile ($Q_1$) $= \frac{7+1}{2} = 4^{\text{th}}$ value of the lower half.
    $Q_1 = 32$. 
    
<marks> M1 </marks>: Upper half of data (values above the median): $39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48$.

Position of upper quartile ($Q_3$) $= \frac{7+1}{2} = 4^{\text{th}}$ value of the upper half.
    

<marks> A1 </marks>: $Q_3 = 42$.

steps.title: Sort the data
steps.content:
First, list the scores in ascending order to prepare for finding the median and quartiles.

$$25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36, 38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48$$

gdc.instructions: In Statistics mode, enter data into List 1. Select 'Sort' or 'Tool' > 'Sort Ascending'.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [1:Edit]. Enter values into L1. Press [STAT] > [2:SortA(] > [2nd] [1] (L1) > [ENTER]. View L1 to see sorted data.

desmos.instructions: Type `L = [32, 45, ...]` and then `sort(L)` to see the ordered list.

calculator.description: Enter the data into a list to sort it automatically.

steps.title: Split the data at the median
steps.content:
Since there are 15 students ($n=15$), the median is the middle value at position $\frac{15+1}{2} = 8$.

The $8^{\text{th}}$ value is $38$. This value splits the data into a lower half (values to the left) and an upper half (values to the right).

steps.title: Find the lower quartile (Q1)
steps.content:
The **lower half** consists of the $7$ values strictly below the median:

$$25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36$$

The lower quartile ($Q_1$) is the median of this lower half. Since there are 7 values, $Q_1$ is the $\frac{7+1}{2} = 4^{\text{th}}$ value of this subset.

$$Q_1 = 32$$

steps.title: Find the upper quartile (Q3)
steps.content:
The **upper half** consists of the $7$ values strictly above the median:

$$39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48$$

The upper quartile ($Q_3$) is the median of this upper half. Since there are 7 values, $Q_3$ is the $\frac{7+1}{2} = 4^{\text{th}}$ value of this subset.

$$Q_3 = 42$$

steps.title: Verify with calculator
steps.content: You can verify these values using the 1-Variable Statistics function on your calculator. This confirms the quartiles without manual sorting errors.

gdc.instructions: In Statistics mode, choose [CALC] > [1-VAR]. Look for Q1 and Q3 in the output.

ti84.instructions: After entering data in L1, press [STAT] > [CALC] > [1:1-Var Stats]. Scroll down to find Q1 and Q3.

desmos.instructions: Type `quartile(L, 1)` for Q1 and `quartile(L, 3)` for Q3.

calculator.description: Calculate 1-Variable Statistics to find Q1 and Q3.

steps.title: Input the data
steps.content: To find the quartiles efficiently, enter the raw data into a list on your Graphic Display Calculator (GDC). There are 15 data points in total.

steps.title: Calculate 1-variable statistics
steps.content: Run the **1-Variable Statistics** command on your calculator. This function processes the list to find key summary statistics, including the median and quartiles, without needing to sort the data manually.

gdc.instructions: Select [CALC] (F2) > [1-VAR] (F1). Ensure the X-List is set to List 1.

steps.title: Read the quartile values
steps.content:
Scroll down the results screen on your calculator to find the lower quartile ($Q_1$) and the upper quartile ($Q_3$).

The calculator output shows:
- **Q1** (or Q$_1$) $= 32$
- **Q3** (or Q$_3$) $= 42$

These values correspond to the 4th value and the 12th value of the sorted dataset respectively.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The lower quartile is 32 and the upper quartile is 42.

$$\begin{aligned} Q_1 &= 32 \\ Q_3 &= 42 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Calculate the interquartile range of the scores.
parts.markscheme:
Interquartile Range (IQR) $= Q_3 - Q_1$. 

<marks> A1 </marks>: IQR $= 42 - 32 = 10$.

steps.title: Recall the definition of IQR
steps.content:
To calculate the interquartile range (IQR), we need to find the difference between the upper quartile ($Q_3$) and the lower quartile ($Q_1$).

$$ \text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 $$

steps.title: Sort the data
steps.content:
First, arrange the $15$ scores in ascending order (from smallest to largest). It is helpful to cross them off the original list as you write them down to ensure none are missed.

Original: $32, 45, 28, 39, 41, 35, 25, 48, 30, 42, 38, 36, 40, 33, 44$

Sorted:
$$ 25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36, 38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48 $$

steps.title: Find the median to split data
steps.content:
Since there are $n = 15$ data points (an odd number), the median is the value at the exact center position, which is the $\frac{15+1}{2} = 8$th value.

$$ 25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36, \textbf{38}, 39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48 $$

The median is $38$. This value splits the data into a lower half and an upper half.

steps.title: Determine the lower quartile (Q1)
steps.content:
The lower quartile, $Q_1$, is the median of the **lower half** of the data (the values to the left of the median).

Lower half: $$ 25, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36 $$

There are $7$ values in this lower half. The median is the middle value (the 4th value).

$$ Q_1 = 32 $$

steps.title: Determine the upper quartile (Q3)
steps.content:
The upper quartile, $Q_3$, is the median of the **upper half** of the data (the values to the right of the median).

Upper half: $$ 39, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48 $$

There are $7$ values in this upper half. The median is the middle value (the 4th value in this group).

$$ Q_3 = 42 $$

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
Now, subtract $Q_1$ from $Q_3$ to find the interquartile range.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{IQR} &= Q_3 - Q_1 \\ &= 42 - 32 \\ &= 10 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify with GDC (Optional)
steps.content:
You can verify this using the 1-Variable Statistics function on your calculator.

1. Enter the data into a list.
2. Run **1-Var Stats**.
3. Check the output for $Q_1=32$ and $Q_3=42$.
4. Confirm $\text{IQR} = 10$.

questions.specification: Consider four real numbers $a$, $b$, $c$, and $d$ such that $a<b<c<d$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) uses relationships between mean, median, range, and maximum for a set of four ordered real numbers to determine unknown values given central tendency constraints.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Number and Algebra > SL 1.3—Geometric sequences and series > Question Type 3: Working with geometric sequences algebraically
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Working with geometric sequences algebraically
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) imposes that the four numbers form a geometric sequence and asks for the median, requiring algebraic work with geometric sequences (terms and ratio).

parts.content: Let $a<b<c<d$ where the maximum is twice the range, and the median is 10. Find the value of $a$ for which the mean is 11.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that the median is 10: $\frac{b+c}{2}=10 \Rightarrow b+c=20$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State relationship between maximum and range: $d=2(d-a) \Rightarrow d=2a$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute into mean formula: $\frac{a+b+c+d}{4}=11 \Rightarrow \frac{a+20+2a}{4}=11$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $a$: $20+3a=44 \Rightarrow 3a=24 \Rightarrow a=8$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the median condition
steps.content:
First, we look at the condition regarding the median. For four ordered numbers $a < b < c < d$, the median is the average of the two middle terms ($b$ and $c$).

Given the median is 10:
$$\frac{b+c}{2} = 10$$

Multiply both sides by 2 to find the sum of the middle terms:
$$b+c = 20$$

steps.title: Relate maximum and range
steps.content:
Next, we express the relationship between the maximum value ($d$) and the range ($d-a$). The question states the maximum is twice the range.

$$d = 2(d-a)$$

We simplify this equation to find $d$ in terms of $a$:
$$\begin{aligned} d &= 2d - 2a \\ 2a &= 2d - d \\ d &= 2a \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into mean formula
steps.content:
The mean of the four numbers is 11. The formula for the mean is:
$$\frac{a + b + c + d}{4} = 11$$

Now we use the **Algebraic Substitution** method. We substitute $(b+c) = 20$ and $d = 2a$ directly into this equation:
$$\frac{a + 20 + 2a}{4} = 11$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
Now we solve the linear equation for $a$.

First, multiply both sides by 4:
$$a + 20 + 2a = 44$$

Combine the terms with $a$:
$$\begin{aligned} 3a + 20 &= 44 \\ 3a &= 24 \\ a &= 8 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of a directly:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of a directly:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of a directly:

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The value of $a$ is 8.

steps.title: Analyze the median
steps.content:
First, we look at the definition of the median for an ordered data set with an even number of terms ($n=4$). The median is the mean of the two middle terms, $b$ and $c$.

Since the median is 10, we can write:

$$ \frac{b+c}{2} = 10 $$

Multiplying both sides by 2 gives the sum of the middle terms:

$$ b+c = 20 $$

steps.title: Find the total sum
steps.content:
The question states the mean of the four numbers is 11. We can use the formula for the mean:

$$ \text{Mean} = \frac{\text{Sum of terms}}{\text{Number of terms}} $$

$$ 11 = \frac{a+b+c+d}{4} $$

Multiplying by 4 gives the total sum:

$$ a+b+c+d = 44 $$

steps.title: Calculate sum of extremes
steps.content:
Using the **Sum of Extremes Approach**, we isolate the sum of the outer terms ($a$ and $d$) by subtracting the sum of the middle terms ($b+c$) from the total sum.

$$\begin{aligned} a+d &= (a+b+c+d) - (b+c) \\ a+d &= 44 - 20 \\ a+d &= 24 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Subtract sum of middle terms from total sum

steps.title: Apply the range condition
steps.content:
The problem states that the maximum ($d$) is twice the range ($d-a$). We can write this as an equation and simplify it to find a relationship between $a$ and $d$.

$$\begin{aligned} d &= 2(\text{range}) \\ d &= 2(d-a) \\ d &= 2d - 2a \\ 2a &= d \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve the system for a
steps.content:
Now we have a system of two linear equations involving the extreme terms $a$ and $d$:

$$\begin{aligned} (1) \quad a + d &= 24 \\ (2) \quad d &= 2a \end{aligned}$$

Substitute the expression for $d$ from the second equation into the first equation:

$$\begin{aligned} a + (2a) &= 24 \\ 3a &= 24 \\ a &= 8 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Divide by 3 to solve for a

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The value of $a$ is 8.

To double check: if $a=8$, then $d=16$. Range is $16-8=8$. Maximum is $16$, which is indeed twice the range ($2 \times 8 = 16$). The sum $a+d = 8+16=24$, which matches our calculation.

parts.content: Let $a$ be the value found in part 1, and $d$ be the corresponding maximum value. If $a, b, c, d$ form a geometric sequence, find the median.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify parameters for geometric sequence: $u_1=a=8$ and $u_4=d=16$

- Find common ratio: $16 = 8r^3 \Rightarrow r^3=2 \Rightarrow r=\sqrt[3]{2}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Determine terms $b$ and $c$: $b=8\sqrt[3]{2}$ and $c=8(\sqrt[3]{2})^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate median: $m=\frac{b+c}{2} = \frac{8\sqrt[3]{2} + 8(\sqrt[3]{2})^2}{2} = \frac{8\sqrt[3]{2}(1+\sqrt[3]{2})}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify the sequence parameters
steps.content:
From Part 1, we established that $a = 8$. The question states that $d = 16$ is the maximum value. Since $a, b, c, d$ form a **geometric sequence** with $a < b < c < d$, we can assign these values to the first and fourth terms:

$$\begin{aligned} u_1 &= a = 8 \\ u_4 &= d = 16 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for the common ratio
steps.content:
We use the geometric sequence formula from the **Sequences and Series** section of the data booklet to find the common ratio $r$.

$$u_{n}=u_{1}r^{n-1}$$

Substitute $n=4$, $u_1=8$, and $u_4=16$:

$$\begin{aligned} 16 &= 8 r^{4-1} \\ 16 &= 8 r^3 \\ 2 &= r^3 \\ r &= \sqrt[3]{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the middle terms
steps.content:
Now we calculate the middle terms $b$ ($u_2$) and $c$ ($u_3$) using the common ratio $r = \sqrt[3]{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} b &= u_1 r = 8(\sqrt[3]{2}) \\ c &= u_1 r^2 = 8(\sqrt[3]{2})^2 = 8\sqrt[3]{4} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the median
steps.content:
Since there are an even number of terms (4), the median is the mean of the two middle terms, $b$ and $c$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Median} &= \frac{b + c}{2} \\ &= \frac{8\sqrt[3]{2} + 8(\sqrt[3]{2})^2}{2} \\ &= \frac{8\sqrt[3]{2} + 8\sqrt[3]{4}}{2} \\ &= 4\sqrt[3]{2} + 4\sqrt[3]{4} \end{aligned}$$

This can also be factored as:

$$4\sqrt[3]{2}(1 + \sqrt[3]{2})$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the mean of the two middle terms

ti84.instructions: Calculate the mean of the two middle terms

desmos.instructions: Calculate the mean of the two middle terms

steps.title: Express a and d as powers
steps.content:
First, we identify the first term $a$ and the last term $d$ given in the problem instructions and previous parts. We are using the **Powers of 2 Approach**, so we rewrite these values as powers of 2.

From the method description:
- $a = 8 = 2^3$
- $d = 16 = 2^4$

Since $a, b, c, d$ form a geometric sequence, their exponents will form an **arithmetic sequence**.

highlights.text: Recognize that $a=8$ and $d=16$ are powers of 2 ($2^3$ and $2^4$)

steps.title: Find the exponent difference
steps.content:
Let the exponents of the geometric sequence be $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$. These form an arithmetic sequence where:
- $u_1 = 3$ (exponent of $a$)
- $u_4 = 4$ (exponent of $d$)

We use the arithmetic sequence formula from the data booklet to find the common difference of the exponents, let's call it $\delta$.

$$u_n = u_1 + (n-1)d$$

Substituting our values for the 4th term:
$$\begin{aligned} 4 &= 3 + (4-1)\delta \\ 1 &= 3\delta \\ \delta &= \frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine middle terms b and c
steps.content:
Now we can find the exponents for the middle terms $b$ and $c$ by adding the difference $\delta = \frac{1}{3}$ to the previous exponent.

**For $b$ (2nd term):**
Exponent $= 3 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{10}{3}$
$$b = 2^{10/3}$$

**For $c$ (3rd term):**
Exponent $= \frac{10}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{11}{3}$
$$c = 2^{11/3}$$

Converting these to radical form makes them easier to work with:
$$\begin{aligned} b &= 2^3 \cdot 2^{1/3} = 8\sqrt[3]{2} \\ c &= 2^3 \cdot 2^{2/3} = 8\sqrt[3]{4} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: find the middle terms as $2^{10/3}$ and $2^{11/3}$

steps.title: Set up the median formula
steps.content:
For a set of four numbers ordered $a < b < c < d$, the median is the average (mean) of the two middle numbers, $b$ and $c$.

$$m = \frac{b + c}{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the median
steps.content:
Substitute the values of $b$ and $c$ into the median formula:

$$m = \frac{8\sqrt[3]{2} + 8\sqrt[3]{4}}{2}$$

Divide each term by 2:
$$m = 4\sqrt[3]{2} + 4\sqrt[3]{4}$$

We can factor out $4\sqrt[3]{2}$ to match the form often seen in markschemes:
$$m = 4\sqrt[3]{2}(1 + \sqrt[3]{2})$$

This matches the result if we used the standard geometric ratio method.

questions.specification: A dataset consists of the numbers $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}$, with mean $\bar{x}$ and standard deviation $s$.

parts.content: If each number is increased by a constant $c$, show that the new mean is $\bar{x}+c$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify new data points as $x_i + c$ and set up the mean formula: $\text{New mean} = \frac{\sum (x_i + c)}{n}$ <marks>M1</marks>

 - Expand and simplify: $\frac{\sum x_i + nc}{n} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} + c = \bar{x} + c$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the original mean
steps.content:
First, let's express the mean of the original dataset, $\bar{x}$, using sigma notation. This represents the sum of all data points divided by the number of points, $n$.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$$

steps.title: Set up the new mean
steps.content:
Since each number is increased by a constant $c$, the new data points are $(x_1 + c), (x_2 + c), \dots, (x_n + c)$. We calculate the new mean by summing these new values and dividing by $n$.

$$\text{New mean} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i + c)}{n}$$

steps.title: Split the sigma summation
steps.content:
Using the properties of sigma notation, we can split the sum of terms into two separate sums: one for the variable $x_i$ and one for the constant $c$.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i + c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} c$$

steps.title: Evaluate the constant sum
steps.content:
The term $\sum_{i=1}^{n} c$ represents adding the constant $c$ to itself $n$ times (once for each data point). This simplifies to $n$ multiplied by $c$.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} c = \underbrace{c + c + \dots + c}_{n \text{ times}} = nc$$

steps.title: Substitute and split fraction
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the expanded numerator back into our expression for the new mean and split the fraction into two parts.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New mean} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + nc}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n} + \frac{nc}{n} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify to final result
steps.content:
Finally, we recognize that $\frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ is the original mean $\bar{x}$, and we cancel the $n$ in the second term.

$$\text{New mean} = \bar{x} + c$$

steps.title: Recall the mean definition
steps.content:
First, let's write down the formula for the mean of the original dataset. The mean $\bar{x}$ is the sum of all individual data points divided by the total count $n$.

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n}{n} $$

steps.title: Write the new mean sum
steps.content:
Since each number is **increased by a constant** $c$, our new data points are $(x_1 + c), (x_2 + c), \dots, (x_n + c)$. We write the expression for the new mean by summing these new terms and dividing by $n$:

$$ \text{New Mean} = \frac{(x_1 + c) + (x_2 + c) + \dots + (x_n + c)}{n} $$

steps.title: Group variables and constants
steps.content:
In the numerator, we can use the commutative property of addition to group all the $x$ terms together and all the $c$ terms together. This separates the variable part from the constant part.

$$ \text{New Mean} = \frac{(x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n) + (c + c + \dots + c)}{n} $$

steps.title: Simplify the constant sum
steps.content:
Since there are $n$ data points, the constant $c$ is added $n$ times. We can simplify the sum of constants as $nc$.

$$ \text{New Mean} = \frac{(x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n) + nc}{n} $$

steps.title: Split the fraction
steps.content:
We can split the single fraction into two separate fractions with the common denominator $n$.

$$ \text{New Mean} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n}{n} + \frac{nc}{n} $$

steps.title: Final simplification
steps.content:
Now we recognize the two parts:
1. $\frac{x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n}{n}$ is the original mean $\bar{x}$.
2. $\frac{nc}{n}$ simplifies to just $c$.

Substituting these back gives the final result:

$$ \text{New Mean} = \bar{x} + c $$

steps.title: Verify with an example
steps.content:
You can verify this property numerically with your calculator. Let's take a simple set like $\{2, 4, 6\}$ (mean is 4) and add 5 to each term.

Original: $\text{Mean} = \frac{2+4+6}{3} = 4$
New set: $\{7, 9, 11\} \rightarrow \text{Mean} = \frac{7+9+11}{3} = 9$

Notice that $9 = 4 + 5$, confirming the rule.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Spreadsheet
2. Enter 2, 4, 6 in Col A
3. In Col B formula bar, type =A[]+5
4. Use One-Variable Statistics on Col A (result 4) and Col B (result 9)

4. Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [1:1-Var Stats] for L1 (x̄=4)
5. Repeat for L2 (x̄=9)

2. Type mean(L) -> returns 4
3. Type mean(L + 5) -> returns 9

calculator.description: Verify the mean property with a list

steps.title: Recall the definition of the mean
steps.content:
First, let's write down the formula for the mean of the original dataset. The mean $\bar{x}$ is defined as the sum of all data points divided by the number of points, $n$.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n}{n}$$

This implies that the **total sum** of the original data is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = n\bar{x}$$

steps.title: Calculate the new total sum
steps.content:
Now, consider the new dataset where every number is increased by a constant $c$. The new data points are $(x_1 + c), (x_2 + c), \dots, (x_n + c)$.

Using the **Total Sum Approach**, we find the sum of these new values. Since we are adding $c$ to each of the $n$ data points, the total sum increases by $n \times c$.

$$\text{New Sum} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i + c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \underbrace{c + c + \dots + c}_{n \text{ times}}$$

$$\text{New Sum} = \sum x_i + nc$$

highlights.text: Identify new data points as $x_i + c$

steps.title: Divide by n to find new mean
steps.content:
To find the new mean, we divide the new total sum by the number of data points, $n$. This step is crucial for showing the relationship required by the question.

$$\text{New Mean} = \frac{\text{New Sum}}{n} = \frac{\sum x_i + nc}{n}$$

We can split this fraction into two parts:

$$\text{New Mean} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} + \frac{nc}{n}$$

steps.title: Simplify to the final result
steps.content:
Finally, we simplify the expression. We know from Step 0 that $\frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{x}$, and the $n$ terms in the second fraction cancel out.

$$\text{New Mean} = \bar{x} + c$$

This confirms that adding a constant to every data point simply shifts the mean by that same constant.

steps.title: Verify with a numerical example
steps.content: While the algebraic proof is required, you can verify this property on your calculator using a simple list of numbers to build your intuition.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Statistics mode.
2. Enter 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in List 1.
3. Calculate 1-Var stats for List 1 to see mean = 3.
4. In the formula bar for List 2, type List 1 + 10.
5. Calculate 1-Var stats for List 2.
6. Confirm the new mean is 13.

4. Go back to [STAT] > Edit. Move cursor to the header L2.
5. Type [L1] + 10 and press [ENTER]. This adds 10 to all values.
6. Press [STAT] > Calc > 1-Var Stats. Ensure List is set to L2.
7. Observe the new mean x̄ = 13 (which is 3 + 10).

1. Type L = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
2. Type mean(L) to get 3.
3. Type mean(L + 10).
4. Confirm the result is 13.

parts.content: If each number is increased by $c$, show that the new standard deviation is $s$.
parts.markscheme:
- Set up the new variance formula: $\text{New variance} = \frac{\sum ((x_i + c) - (\bar{x} + c))^2}{n}$ <marks>M1</marks>

 - Simplify to show it equals original variance: $\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} = s^2$, therefore standard deviation is $s$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State the original variance formula
steps.content:
First, recall the definition of variance for the original dataset $x_1, \ldots, x_n$. The variance $s^2$ is the average of the squared deviations from the mean $\bar{x}$.

$$s^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Define the new values
steps.content:
The question states that each number is increased by $c$. Let the new values be $y_i$ and the new mean be $\bar{y}$.

$$\begin{aligned} y_i &= x_i + c \\ \bar{y} &= \bar{x} + c \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: each number is increased by c

steps.title: Substitute into variance formula
steps.content:
Now, we substitute these new values into the variance formula. This step is crucial for establishing the relationship between the new and original variance.

$$\text{New Variance} = \frac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n} = \frac{\sum ((x_i + c) - (\bar{x} + c))^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Simplify the expression inside the squared brackets. Notice that the constant $c$ cancels out.

$$\begin{aligned} (x_i + c) - (\bar{x} + c) &= x_i + c - \bar{x} - c \\ &= x_i - \bar{x} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclude the proof
steps.content:
Substitute the simplified term back into the summation. We can see that the new variance is identical to the original variance.

$$\text{New Variance} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} = s^2$$ 

Since the variance is unchanged ($s^2$), the new standard deviation is also $s$.

steps.title: Verify with a numerical example
steps.content:
Let's verify this property with a simple example using the calculator. 

Consider the dataset $\{2, 4, 6\}$ with mean $4$. If we add $c=5$ to each number, we get $\{7, 9, 11\}$ with mean $9$. The standard deviation should remain approximately $1.63$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Statistics mode
2. Enter {2, 4, 6} in List 1
3. Enter {7, 9, 11} in List 2
4. Select Calc > 1-Variable for List 1
5. Observe sigma_x
6. Repeat for List 2

2. Enter {2, 4, 6} in L1
3. Enter {7, 9, 11} in L2
4. Press [STAT] > CALC > 1-Var Stats L1
5. Observe sigma_x (population std dev)
6. Repeat for L2

2. Type stdevp([7,9,11]) to verify it matches

calculator.description: Calculate standard deviation for original and shifted data

steps.title: Define the new dataset variables
steps.content:
Let the new dataset values be $y_i = x_i + c$. 

Since every value is increased by $c$, the new mean $\bar{y}$ will also shift by $c$:
$$\bar{y} = \bar{x} + c$$

steps.title: State the computational variance formula
steps.content:
We use the computational formula for variance, which is often easier for algebraic proofs. For the new dataset $y$, the variance $s_{new}^2$ is:

$$s_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum y_i^2}{n} - \bar{y}^2$$

steps.title: Substitute the expressions
steps.content:
Substitute $y_i = x_i + c$ and $\bar{y} = \bar{x} + c$ into the formula:

$$s_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i + c)^2}{n} - (\bar{x} + c)^2$$

steps.title: Expand the squared terms
steps.content:
First, expand the quadratic terms inside the summation and the mean term:

$$s_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i^2 + 2cx_i + c^2)}{n} - (\bar{x}^2 + 2c\bar{x} + c^2)$$

steps.title: Distribute the summation
steps.content:
We can break the summation into three separate parts. Recall that $\sum c^2 = nc^2$ because $c^2$ is added $n$ times:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\sum (x_i^2 + 2cx_i + c^2)}{n} &= \frac{\sum x_i^2 + 2c\sum x_i + nc^2}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} + \frac{2c\sum x_i}{n} + \frac{nc^2}{n} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify using the mean
steps.content:
Recognize that $\frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{x}$. We can simplify the expression from the previous step:

$$\frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} + 2c\bar{x} + c^2$$

steps.title: Combine and cancel terms
steps.content:
Now substitute this back into our variance equation and subtract the expanded mean squared term $(\bar{x}^2 + 2c\bar{x} + c^2)$:

$$\begin{aligned} s_{new}^2 &= \left( \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} + 2c\bar{x} + c^2 \right) - (\bar{x}^2 + 2c\bar{x} + c^2) \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} + 2c\bar{x} + c^2 - \bar{x}^2 - 2c\bar{x} - c^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the original variance
steps.content:
Notice that the terms with $c$ cancel out completely ($2c\bar{x} - 2c\bar{x} = 0$ and $c^2 - c^2 = 0$):

$$s_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2$$

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
The result is exactly the formula for the original variance $s^2$.

Since $s_{new}^2 = s^2$, the new standard deviation is:
$$s_{new} = s$$

parts.content: If each number is multiplied by a constant $k$ (where $k>0$ ), show that the new variance is $k^{2} s^{2}$.
parts.markscheme:
- New data: $k x_{i}$ <marks>M1</marks>

 - New mean $= k \bar{x}$ <marks>M1</marks>

 - Substitute into variance formula: $\text{New variance} = \frac{\sum (k x_{i} - k \bar{x})^2}{n} = \frac{k^2 \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} = k^2 s^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Recall the variance formula
steps.content:
First, let's write down the definition of the variance, $s^2$, for the original dataset using the mean deviation formula. This formula measures the average squared difference from the mean $\bar{x}$:

$$s^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Identify new data and mean
steps.content:
We are told that each number in the dataset is multiplied by a constant $k$. Let's denote the new data points as $y_i$ and the new mean as $\bar{y}$.

The new data values are:
$$y_i = k x_i$$

The new mean is simply the original mean multiplied by $k$:
$$\bar{y} = k \bar{x}$$

highlights.text: each number is multiplied by a constant $k$

steps.title: Substitute into variance formula
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the new values ($y_i$) and the new mean ($\bar{y}$) into the variance formula to find the new variance.

$$\text{New Variance} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (k x_i - k \bar{x})^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Factor out the constant
steps.content:
Inside the squared term, we can factor out $k$. Remember that when you square the product, the constant becomes squared as well:

$$(k x_i - k \bar{x})^2 = (k(x_i - \bar{x}))^2 = k^2(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Substitute this back into our variance equation. Since $k^2$ is a constant factor common to every term in the summation, we can pull it out in front of the sum:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New Variance} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n k^2(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \\ &= \frac{k^2 \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \\ &= k^2 \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
We recognize the term inside the brackets is exactly the definition of the original variance $s^2$. Therefore, the new variance is:

$$\text{New Variance} = k^2 s^2$$

steps.title: Define the new dataset
steps.content:
Let the original data values be denoted by $x_i$. If each number is multiplied by a constant $k$, the new data values $y_i$ are:

$$y_i = k x_i$$

steps.title: Calculate the new mean
steps.content:
The mean of the new dataset, $\bar{y}$, is calculated by multiplying the original mean by $k$:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n} = \frac{\sum k x_i}{n} = k \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = k \bar{x}$$

steps.title: Apply mean of squares formula
steps.content:
Using the alternative variance formula (mean of squares minus the square of the mean) for the new dataset:

$$s_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum y_i^2}{n} - (\bar{y})^2$$

steps.title: Substitute and expand
steps.content:
Substitute $y_i = k x_i$ and $\bar{y} = k \bar{x}$ into the formula:

$$s_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum (k x_i)^2}{n} - (k \bar{x})^2$$

Expand the squared terms:

$$s_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum k^2 x_i^2}{n} - k^2 \bar{x}^2$$

steps.title: Factor out constant
steps.content:
Since $k^2$ is a common factor in both terms, we can factor it out:

$$\begin{aligned} s_{new}^2 &= k^2 \left( \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} \right) - k^2 \bar{x}^2 \\ &= k^2 \left( \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2 \right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify original variance
steps.content:
The expression inside the brackets is the formula for the original variance $s^2$. Therefore:

$$s_{new}^2 = k^2 s^2$$

questions.specification: A dataset consists of $n$ values $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}$, with mean $\bar{x}$ and variance $s^{2}$. A new dataset is created by applying the transformation $y_{i}=a x_{i}+b$, where $a>0$ and $b$ are constants.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 7: Determining the effect on mean, mode, median, range, variance, standard deviation and IQR given specific transformations of data
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 7: Determining the effect on mean, mode, median, range, variance, standard deviation and IQR given specific transformations of data
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts concern how summary statistics (mean, variance/standard deviation, and IQR/quartiles) change under a linear transformation y=ax+b, including a numerical application—this matches the transformation-effects leaf topic.

parts.content: Show that the mean of the new dataset is $a \bar{x}+b$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to substitute expression for $y_i$ into mean formula: $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum (a x_i + b)}{n}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Separate terms and simplify: $= a \frac{\sum x_i}{n} + \frac{nb}{n} = a \bar{x} + b$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State the definition of the mean
steps.content:
First, let's write down the definition of the mean for the new dataset $y$. The mean is the sum of all values divided by the number of values $n$.

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n} $$

steps.title: Substitute the expression for y
steps.content:
The question tells us that each value in the new dataset is related to the old dataset by the linear transformation $y_i = a x_i + b$. We substitute this expression into our mean formula.

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (a x_i + b)}{n} $$

steps.title: Separate the summation
steps.content:
Using the properties of sigma notation (summation), we can split the sum into two separate parts. 

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} b}{n} $$

steps.title: Simplify the sums
steps.content:
Now we simplify each part of the numerator:
1. For the first term, the constant $a$ can be factored out of the summation: $\sum a x_i = a \sum x_i$.
2. For the second term, we are summing the constant $b$, $n$ times: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} b = \underbrace{b + b + \dots + b}_{n \text{ times}} = nb$.

Substituting these back gives:

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{a \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + nb}{n} $$

steps.title: Divide and identify the original mean
steps.content:
Finally, we split the fraction into two terms and simplify. We recognize that $\frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ is the definition of the original mean, $\bar{x}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= \frac{a \sum x_i}{n} + \frac{nb}{n} \\ &= a \left( \frac{\sum x_i}{n} \right) + b \\ &= a \bar{x} + b \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Define the new mean
steps.content:
To find the mean of the new dataset, we start with the definition of the arithmetic mean. It is the sum of all individual values divided by the total number of values $n$.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{y_1 + y_2 + \dots + y_n}{n}$$

steps.title: Substitute the transformation
steps.content:
We are given that each new value is created by the transformation $y_i = a x_i + b$. We substitute this expression into our mean formula for every term.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{(a x_1 + b) + (a x_2 + b) + \dots + (a x_n + b)}{n}$$

steps.title: Group like terms
steps.content:
Next, we rearrange the numerator to group all terms containing $x$ together and all constant terms $b$ together.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{(a x_1 + a x_2 + \dots + a x_n) + (b + b + \dots + b)}{n}$$

steps.title: Factor and count constants
steps.content:
We can factor out the constant $a$ from the $x$ terms. Also, since there are $n$ data points, the constant $b$ is added $n$ times.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{a(x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n) + nb}{n}$$

steps.title: Separate the fraction
steps.content:
Now, we split the fraction into two separate parts and simplify the term with $b$.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{a(x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n)}{n} + \frac{nb}{n}$$

steps.title: Identify original mean
steps.content:
Finally, we recognize that the fraction involving $x$ is the definition of the original mean $\bar{x}$. We replace it to get the final result.

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= a \left( \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \dots + x_n}{n} \right) + b \\ &= a \bar{x} + b \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Show that the variance of the new dataset is $a^{2} s^{2}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into variance formula: $\text{Variance} = \frac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n} = \frac{\sum (a x_i + b - (a \bar{x} + b))^2}{n}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Simplify expression inside summation: $= \frac{\sum (a(x_i - \bar{x}))^2}{n}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Factor out constant to show result: $= a^2 \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} = a^2 s^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: State the variance definition
steps.content:
To show the relationship, we start with the fundamental definition of variance for the new dataset $y$. The variance is defined as the mean of the squared deviations from the mean:

$$\text{Variance}_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Express the new mean
steps.content:
First, we must express the mean of the new dataset, $\bar{y}$, in terms of $\bar{x}$. Since every value is transformed linearly by $y_i = ax_i + b$, the mean is transformed in the same way:

$$\bar{y} = a\bar{x} + b$$

steps.title: Substitute into variance formula
steps.content:
Substitute the expressions for $y_i$ and $\bar{y}$ into the variance formula from Step 0.

$$\text{Variance}_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} ((a x_i + b) - (a \bar{x} + b))^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Simplify the term inside
steps.content:
Simplify the expression inside the squared brackets. Note that the constant $b$ cancels out:

$$\begin{aligned} (a x_i + b) - (a \bar{x} + b) &= a x_i + b - a \bar{x} - b \\ &= a x_i - a \bar{x} \\ &= a(x_i - \bar{x}) \end{aligned}$$

Substituting this back into our variance equation:

$$\text{Variance}_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (a(x_i - \bar{x}))^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Factor out the constant
steps.content:
Apply the square to the terms inside the parentheses, $(ab)^2 = a^2b^2$. Since $a^2$ is a constant coefficient for every term in the summation, we can factor it out in front of the summation sign:

$$\text{Variance}_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a^2 (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} = a^2 \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \right)$$

steps.title: Identify the original variance
steps.content:
We recognize the term in the brackets, $\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}$, as the definition of the variance of the original dataset, $s^2$. Substituting this back gives the final result:

$$\text{Variance}_y = a^2 s^2$$

steps.title: State the computational variance formula
steps.content:
To solve this using the computational formula approach, we use the alternative formula for variance provided in **Topic 4.3 (Statistics)**. For a dataset $y$, the variance is given by:

$$\text{Var}(y) = \frac{\sum y_i^2}{n} - (\bar{y})^2$$

We are given the transformation $y_i = ax_i + b$. The mean of the new dataset transforms linearly, so $\bar{y} = a\bar{x} + b$.

steps.title: Expand the squared sum term
steps.content:
First, we examine the $\sum y_i^2$ term by substituting $y_i = ax_i + b$ and expanding the quadratic:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum y_i^2 &= \sum (ax_i + b)^2 \\ &= \sum (a^2 x_i^2 + 2ab x_i + b^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Distribute the summation
steps.content:
We distribute the summation operation across the terms. Recall that $\sum x_i = n\bar{x}$ and the sum of a constant $n$ times is $\sum b^2 = nb^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum (a^2 x_i^2 + 2ab x_i + b^2) &= a^2 \sum x_i^2 + 2ab \sum x_i + \sum b^2 \\ &= a^2 \sum x_i^2 + 2ab(n\bar{x}) + nb^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into variance equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute this expanded sum and the expression for $\bar{y}$ back into our variance formula. Note that the $\frac{1}{n}$ term divides each part of the sum:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(y) &= \frac{a^2 \sum x_i^2 + 2ab n\bar{x} + nb^2}{n} - (a\bar{x} + b)^2 \\ &= \left( a^2 \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} + 2ab\bar{x} + b^2 \right) - (a\bar{x} + b)^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Expand and cancel cross-terms
steps.content:
Next, we expand the squared mean term $(a\bar{x} + b)^2 = a^2 \bar{x}^2 + 2ab\bar{x} + b^2$ and subtract it from our previous expression. Notice how the cross-terms and constants cancel out:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(y) &= a^2 \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} + 2ab\bar{x} + b^2 - (a^2 \bar{x}^2 + 2ab\bar{x} + b^2) \\ &= a^2 \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} + 2ab\bar{x} + b^2 - a^2 \bar{x}^2 - 2ab\bar{x} - b^2 \\ &= a^2 \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - a^2 \bar{x}^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Factor to identify original variance
steps.content:
Finally, we factor out $a^2$ and recognize the computational formula for the variance of the original dataset $s^2 = \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(y) &= a^2 \left( \frac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2 \right) \\ &= a^2 s^2 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: If $a=2, b=5$, and the original dataset has $\bar{x}=10$ and $s=4$, calculate the mean and standard deviation of the new dataset.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate new mean: $\text{Mean} = 2(10) + 5 = 25$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate new standard deviation: $\text{Standard deviation} = \sqrt{2^2 \times 4^2} = 8$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify transformation parameters
steps.content:
First, we identify the parameters of the linear transformation from the given equation $y_i = 2x_i + 5$.

Comparing this to the general form $y_i = ax_i + b$:
- The scaling factor is $a = 2$
- The shift constant is $b = 5$

We are given the original statistics:
- Mean $\bar{x} = 10$
- Standard deviation $s_x = 4$

steps.title: Calculate the new mean
steps.content:
According to the **Linear Transformation Rules**, the mean is affected by both the scaling factor $a$ and the shift constant $b$. The rule is:

$$\bar{y} = a\bar{x} + b$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= 2(10) + 5 \\ &= 20 + 5 \\ &= 25 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the new standard deviation
steps.content:
For the standard deviation, adding a constant $b$ simply shifts the data without changing how spread out it is. Therefore, $b$ is ignored. The standard deviation is only affected by the scaling factor $a$.

The rule is:
$$s_y = |a|s_x$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} s_y &= |2| \times 4 \\ &= 2 \times 4 \\ &= 8 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answers
steps.content:
The new statistics for the transformed dataset are:

- **Mean:** $25$
- **Standard deviation:** $8$

steps.title: Setup the mean calculation
steps.content:
To find the new mean $\bar{y}$, we use the algebraic definition of the mean for the transformed data $y_i = ax_i + b$.

Starting with the summation definition:
$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n}$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify mean
steps.content:
We substitute the transformation equation into the sum and separate the terms:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{y} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (ax_i + b)}{n} \\ &= \frac{a\sum x_i + \sum b}{n} \\ &= \frac{a(n\bar{x}) + nb}{n} \\ &= a\bar{x} + b \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the new mean
steps.content:
Now we substitute the given values $a=2$, $b=5$, and $\bar{x}=10$ into our derived formula:

$$\bar{y} = 2(10) + 5 = 25$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the linear transformation of the mean

desmos.instructions: Calculate the linear transformation of the mean

steps.title: Setup the variance calculation
steps.content:
Next, we derive the new variance $s_y^2$ using the definition of variance:
$$s_y^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n}$$

Substitute $y_i = ax_i + b$ and our result $\bar{y} = a\bar{x} + b$:

$$s_y^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} ((ax_i + b) - (a\bar{x} + b))^2}{n}$$

steps.title: Simplify the variance expression
steps.content:
Notice that the constant $b$ cancels out inside the parenthesis. We can then factor out $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} s_y^2 &= \frac{\sum (a(x_i - \bar{x}))^2}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum a^2(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \\ &= a^2 \left( \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \right) \\ &= a^2 s_x^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate new standard deviation
steps.content:
The new standard deviation is the square root of the variance:
$$s_y = \sqrt{a^2 s_x^2} = a s_x$$

Substituting $a=2$ and the original standard deviation $s_x=4$:
$$s_y = 2 \times 4 = 8$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The new mean is 25 and the new standard deviation is 8.

Key Concept: Adding a constant $b$ affects the mean but not the standard deviation. Multiplying by $a$ scales both the mean and the standard deviation.

parts.content: Prove that the interquartile range (IQR) of the new dataset is $a$ times the IQR of the original dataset.
parts.markscheme:
- State effect on quartiles: New $Q_1 = a Q_1 + b$ and New $Q_3 = a Q_3 + b$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute into IQR definition: $\text{New IQR} = (a Q_3 + b) - (a Q_1 + b)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Simplify to prove result: $= a(Q_3 - Q_1) = a \times \text{Original IQR}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the original IQR
steps.content:
First, let's recall the definition of the Interquartile Range (IQR) for the original dataset. It is the difference between the third quartile ($Q_3$) and the first quartile ($Q_1$).

$$\text{Original IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$$

steps.title: Express the new quartiles
steps.content:
Since the transformation is linear ($y_i = ax_i + b$) and $a > 0$, the order of the data points is preserved. This means the position statistics (like quartiles) are transformed in the exact same way as the individual data values.

We can express the new quartiles ($Q'_1$ and $Q'_3$) in terms of the original ones:

$$\begin{aligned} Q'_1 &= aQ_1 + b \\ Q'_3 &= aQ_3 + b \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into new IQR formula
steps.content:
Now, we apply the definition of IQR to the new dataset by substituting the expressions we found in the previous step.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New IQR} &= Q'_3 - Q'_1 \\ &= (aQ_3 + b) - (aQ_1 + b) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Next, we simplify the expression algebraically. Notice that the constant $b$ is added and then subtracted, so it cancels out.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New IQR} &= aQ_3 + b - aQ_1 - b \\ &= aQ_3 - aQ_1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Factor out a
steps.content:
Finally, we factor out $a$ to show the relationship between the new IQR and the original IQR.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New IQR} &= a(Q_3 - Q_1) \\ &= a \times (\text{Original IQR}) \end{aligned}$$

This proves that the IQR of the new dataset is $a$ times the IQR of the original dataset.

steps.title: Recall the definition of IQR
steps.content:
First, remember that the **Interquartile Range (IQR)** is a measure of spread defined as the difference between the third quartile ($Q_3$) and the first quartile ($Q_1$):

$$\text{IQR}_{\text{original}} = Q_3 - Q_1$$

Our goal is to find an expression for the new IQR in terms of the original one.

steps.title: Analyze the transformation properties
steps.content:
The question defines a linear transformation $y_i = a x_i + b$. Let's break down the conceptual effect of each constant on the data's "spread" (width):

1.  **Adding $b$ (Translation):** This shifts every data point by the same amount. It changes the *position* of the values on the number line, but it does **not** change the distance between any two points. It has no effect on spread.
2.  **Multiplying by $a$ (Dilation):** This scales the data. Since $a > 0$, it stretches the distance between points by a factor of $a$. This directly affects the spread.

steps.title: Determine the new quartiles
steps.content:
Since $a > 0$, the order of the data values is preserved (the smallest $x$ becomes the smallest $y$, etc.). This means the quartiles of the new dataset ($Q_1'$ and $Q_3'$) are found by applying the same transformation to the original quartiles:

$$\begin{aligned} Q_1' &= a Q_1 + b \\ Q_3' &= a Q_3 + b \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into the IQR formula
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the IQR of the new dataset by subtracting the new quartiles:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New IQR} &= Q_3' - Q_1' \\ &= (a Q_3 + b) - (a Q_1 + b) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify to prove the result
steps.content:
Finally, we expand and simplify the expression. Notice how the constant $b$ cancels out, confirming our conceptual argument that shifting data doesn't change its spread.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New IQR} &= aQ_3 + b - aQ_1 - b \\ &= aQ_3 - aQ_1 \\ &= a(Q_3 - Q_1) \\ &= a \times \text{Original IQR} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify with a numerical example
steps.content:
It can be helpful to verify this with a simple dataset on your calculator to see the property in action.

Let's test with a dataset $x = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$ and transformation $y = 3x + 5$.
- Original $Q_1 = 3, Q_3 = 9$, so **Original IQR = 6**.
- Transformation ($a=3, b=5$): $y = \{11, 17, 23, 29, 35\}$.
- New $Q_1 = 14, Q_3 = 32$, so **New IQR = 18**.

Since $18 = 3 \times 6$, the property holds.

parts.content: Show that $b$ is the mean of $a$ and $c$.
parts.markscheme:
- Express terms using common difference $d$: $a=a$, $b=a+d$, $c=a+2d$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute into mean formula and simplify: $\text{Mean} = \frac{a+c}{2} = \frac{a+(a+2d)}{2} = \frac{2a+2d}{2} = a+d = b$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Express terms using common difference
steps.content:
Since $a, b, c$ form an arithmetic sequence, we can express the second and third terms using the first term $a$ and the common difference $d$. Using the arithmetic sequence formula $u_n = u_1 + (n-1)d$, where $u_1 = a$:

For the second term ($n=2$):
$$b = a + (2-1)d = a + d$$

For the third term ($n=3$):
$$c = a + (3-1)d = a + 2d$$

steps.title: Substitute into mean formula
steps.content:
To show that $b$ is the mean of $a$ and $c$, we start with the expression for the arithmetic mean of $a$ and $c$ and substitute our expression for $c$:

$$\text{Mean} = \frac{a + c}{2} = \frac{a + (a + 2d)}{2}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Now, we combine like terms in the numerator and simplify the fraction:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{a + a + 2d}{2} &= \frac{2a + 2d}{2} \\ &= \frac{2(a + d)}{2} \\ &= a + d \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
We previously identified that $b = a + d$. Since our simplified mean expression equals $a + d$, we have shown that:

$$\frac{a + c}{2} = b$$

steps.title: Recall arithmetic sequence properties
steps.content:
In an arithmetic sequence, the difference between any consecutive terms is constant. This is often denoted as the common difference, $d$. 

This concept relates to **Topic 1.2: Arithmetic sequences and series**. Because the terms $a$, $b$, and $c$ are consecutive, the "step" from $a$ to $b$ must be exactly the same as the "step" from $b$ to $c$.

steps.title: Equate consecutive differences
steps.content:
Since the common difference is constant, we can set the difference between the first two terms equal to the difference between the last two terms:

$$b - a = c - b$$

steps.title: Rearrange the equation
steps.content:
To show that $b$ is the mean, we need to isolate $b$. We can do this by gathering the $b$ terms on the left side and moving $a$ to the right side.

Add $b$ to both sides and add $a$ to both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} b - a &= c - b \\ b + b &= a + c \\ 2b &= a + c \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for b
steps.content:
Finally, divide both sides by 2 to isolate $b$:

$$b = \frac{a + c}{2}$$ 

This expression, $\frac{a + c}{2}$, is the definition of the arithmetic mean. We have successfully shown that $b$ is the mean of $a$ and $c$.

parts.content: If $2b$ is the range, find the value of $a$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the range for a strictly increasing sequence: $\text{Range} = c - a$. Substitute $b = \frac{a+c}{2}$: $2\left(\frac{a+c}{2}\right) = c - a$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $a$: $a + c = c - a \Rightarrow 2a = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the range
steps.content:
First, we need to establish an expression for the range of the sequence. Since the problem states that $a, b, c$ form a **strictly increasing arithmetic sequence**, the range is the difference between the last term ($c$) and the first term ($a$).

$$\text{Range} = c - a$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
The question specifies that **2b is the range**. We can equate this to our expression from the previous step:

$$2b = c - a$$

steps.title: Use arithmetic mean property
steps.content:
For any three consecutive terms in an arithmetic sequence ($a, b, c$), the middle term $b$ is the arithmetic mean of the outer terms. This relationship allows us to express $2b$ in terms of $a$ and $c$:

$$\begin{aligned} b &= \frac{a + c}{2} \\ 2b &= a + c \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve for a
steps.content:
Now we substitute the expression $a + c$ for $2b$ into our range equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 2b &= c - a \\ a + c &= c - a \end{aligned}$$

Next, we solve for $a$ by cancelling $c$ from both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} a &= -a \\ 2a &= 0 \\ a &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify with an example
steps.content:
Let's verify this result using a test case where $a=0$. If $a=0$ and we choose an arbitrary positive integer for the sequence step, say the sequence is $0, 1, 2$ ($a=0, b=1, c=2$):

*   $\text{Range} = c - a = 2 - 0 = 2$
*   $\text{Value of } 2b = 2(1) = 2$

Since the range equals $2b$, the value $a=0$ is correct.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The value of $a$ is:

$$a = 0$$

steps.title: Express terms using common difference
steps.content:
Since the terms $a, b, c$ form an arithmetic sequence, we can express them using the first term $a$ and a common difference $d$. This allows us to write all variables in terms of just two unknowns.

$$\begin{aligned} b &= a + d \\ c &= a + 2d \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the range
steps.content:
The range of a strictly increasing sequence is the difference between the largest and smallest terms ($c$ and $a$). We simplify this expression using our definitions from the previous step.

$$\text{Range} = c - a = (a + 2d) - a = 2d$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We are given that $2b$ is equal to the range. We substitute our expressions for $b$ and the range into this condition to form an equation.

$$\begin{aligned} 2b &= \text{Range} \\ 2(a + d) &= 2d \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
Now we simplify the equation to solve for $a$.

$$\begin{aligned} 2a + 2d &= 2d \\ 2a &= 0 \\ a &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:
The value of $a$ is $0$. This implies the sequence starts at $0$ (e.g., $0, d, 2d$).

$$a = 0$$

parts.content: A student said that this forms a geometric sequence of common ratio 2. Is that correct? Explain why or why not.
parts.markscheme:
- State the conclusion: No <marks>A1</marks>
- Reason: The first term is $a=0$. A geometric sequence with first term 0 must have all terms equal to 0. Since the sequence is strictly increasing ($d > 0$), the terms are not all 0, so it cannot be geometric. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the terms
steps.content:
According to the student's claim, the sequence is geometric with a common ratio $r = 2$. If the first term is $a$, the first three terms would be:

$$u_1 = a, \quad u_2 = 2a, \quad u_3 = 4a$$

highlights.text: forms a geometric sequence of common ratio 2

steps.title: Test for arithmetic consistency
steps.content:
The question states that $a, b, c$ form an arithmetic sequence. For an arithmetic sequence, the difference between consecutive terms must be constant.

We set the difference between the second and first term equal to the difference between the third and second term:

$$u_2 - u_1 = u_3 - u_2$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
Substitute the geometric terms ($a, 2a, 4a$) into the arithmetic consistency equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 2a - a &= 4a - 2a \\ a &= 2a \\ 0 &= a \end{aligned}$$

This implies that for the sequence to be both arithmetic and geometric (with $r=2$), the first term $a$ must be $0$.

steps.title: Check constraints
steps.content:
If $a = 0$, the sequence becomes $0, 0, 0$. The common difference is:

$$d = 0 - 0 = 0$$

However, the question specifies that the sequence is **strictly increasing**, which means the common difference must be positive ($d > 0$). A sequence of zeros is not strictly increasing.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since the only possible value for $a$ results in a contradiction with the "strictly increasing" condition, the student's claim is incorrect.

**Answer:** No, the student is incorrect.

steps.title: Define terms algebraically
steps.content:
First, let's express the three terms $a$, $b$, and $c$ using the definition of an arithmetic sequence. Let the first term be $a$ and the common difference be $d$.

Using the formula for the $n$-th term:

- First term: $a$
- Second term: $b = a + d$
- Third term: $c = a + 2d$

steps.title: Set geometric ratio condition
steps.content:
The student claims the sequence is geometric with a common ratio $r=2$. This implies that the ratio of consecutive terms must be equal to 2.

We can write this relationship as:

$$\frac{u_2}{u_1} = 2 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{u_3}{u_2} = 2$$

Substituting our arithmetic expressions:

$$\begin{aligned} b = 2a &\implies a + d = 2a \\ c = 2b &\implies a + 2d = 2(a + d) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for first term
steps.content:
Now, we solve the system of equations to find the values of $a$ and $d$. Let's start with the second equation:

$$\begin{aligned} a + 2d &= 2(a + d) \\ a + 2d &= 2a + 2d \\ a &= 2a \\ 0 &= a \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Algebraic verification (mental check or basic entry)

steps.title: Find common difference
steps.content:
Since we found $a = 0$, we substitute this back into our first equation ($a + d = 2a$) to find the common difference $d$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 + d &= 2(0) \\ d &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Check consistency
steps.content:
We have found that for the sequence to be both arithmetic and geometric with ratio 2, we must have $a=0$ and $d=0$. 

However, the question specifies that the sequence is **strictly increasing**. For an arithmetic sequence to be strictly increasing, the common difference must be positive ($d > 0$).

Since $d=0$ contradicts this condition, the student's claim is incorrect.

steps.title: State conclusion
steps.content: Therefore, the student is not correct. A strictly increasing arithmetic sequence cannot form a geometric sequence with a common ratio of 2, as this would require all terms to be zero.

parts.markscheme:

<note>Accept population standard deviations $\sigma_x \approx 8.35$ and $\sigma_y \approx 23.5$. Award A1 for correct means, A1 for correct standard deviations, and A1 for correct correlation coefficient.</note>

steps.title: Enter the data
steps.content:
This question requires the use of the **Statistics** features on your GDC. First, enter the given data into your calculator's lists.

*   Enter the **Protein ($x$)** values into List 1.
*   Enter the **Hunger time ($y$)** values into List 2.

Ensure you enter all 8 pairs correctly.

desmos.instructions: Create a table with columns x_1 and y_1 and enter the data.

steps.title: Calculate mean and standard deviation
steps.content:
To find the mean and standard deviation for both variables, use the **2-Variable Statistics** command. This will calculate all summary statistics at once.

Look for the following symbols in the output:
*   Mean of $x$: $\bar{x}$
*   Sample standard deviation of $x$: $s_x$ (or $Sx$)
*   Mean of $y$: $\bar{y}$
*   Sample standard deviation of $y$: $s_y$ (or $Sy$)

IB Mathematics generally uses the **sample standard deviation** ($s_{n-1}$) unless the question specifies "population".

gdc.instructions:
Select 'Calc' > '2-Var'. Set X List: List 1, Y List: List 2.
Read means and sample standard deviations (sx, sy).

ti84.instructions:
Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [2:2-Var Stats]. Set Xlist: L1, Ylist: L2. Press Calculate.
Read values:
xb = 23
Sx = 8.928...
yb = 98.25
Sy = 25.143...

steps.title: State the means and standard deviations
steps.content:
From the calculator output, write down the values to 3 significant figures (except exact values).

**Means:**
$$\begin{aligned} \bar{x} &= 23 \\ \bar{y} &= 98.25 \end{aligned}$$

**Standard Deviations:**
$$\begin{aligned} s_x &\approx 8.93 \\ s_y &\approx 25.1 \end{aligned}$$

*(Note: The markscheme also accepts population standard deviations $\sigma_x \approx 8.35$ and $\sigma_y \approx 23.5$, but $s_x$ is the standard expectation.)*

steps.title: Find the correlation coefficient
steps.content:
To find the Pearson correlation coefficient $r$, use the **Linear Regression** command (usually $ax+b$).

Ensure your calculator's "Diagnostics" are turned on if $r$ does not appear automatically.

The value of $r$ indicates the strength and direction of the linear relationship.

To estimate the hunger time ($y$) for a specific amount of protein ($x$), we use the regression line equation $y = ax + b$.

- Gradient ($a$) $\approx 2.79$
- Intercept ($b$) $\approx 34.1$

$$y = 2.79x + 34.1$$

steps.content:
Now, perform the calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= 83.7 + 34.1 \\ y &= 117.8 \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to the nearest whole number (or 3 significant figures), we get 118 minutes.
