questions.specification: Consider the following data sets $X$ and $Y$ such that $X=\{x-4, \, x, \, 2x, \, x+9\}$ and $Y=\{y, \, 2y, \, 3y-6\}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 7: Determining the effect on mean, mode, median, range, variance, standard deviation and IQR given specific transformations of data
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 7: Determining the effect on mean, mode, median, range, variance, standard deviation and IQR given specific transformations of data
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for the effect on mean and variance under an additive transformation (+k), which is explicitly about transformations of summary statistics (mean/variance/SD).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (c) involve computing means from parameterized data sets and then using conditions (equal means and x=y) to determine the actual mean value, matching parameter-based central tendency relationships.

parts.content: Find the mean of $X$ and $Y$ in terms of $x$ and $y$ respectively.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the mean of $X$: $\bar{X}=\frac{x-4+x+2x+x+9}{4}=\frac{5x+5}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the mean of $Y$: $\bar{Y}=\frac{y+2y+3y-6}{3}=\frac{6y-6}{3}=2y-2$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the mean formula
steps.content:
This question asks for the arithmetic mean of two data sets. The mean (denoted as $\bar{x}$) is found by summing all the values in the data set and dividing by the number of values ($n$).

$$\text{Mean} = \dfrac{\text{Sum of all elements}}{\text{Number of elements}}$$

This concept is part of **Topic 4: Statistics & Probability**.

steps.title: Calculate the mean of X
steps.content:
First, we determine the number of elements in set $X$. Counting the terms, we see there are $n=4$ values.

Next, we sum the algebraic expressions in the set:
$$\text{Sum}_X = (x-4) + x + 2x + (x+9)$$

Combine the like terms:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum}_X &= (1x + 1x + 2x + 1x) + (-4 + 9) \\ &= 5x + 5 \end{aligned}$$

Now, divide by $n=4$ to find the mean:
$$\bar{X} = \frac{5x+5}{4}$$

steps.title: Calculate the mean of Y
steps.content:
We apply the same process to set $Y$. There are $n=3$ values in this set.

Sum the terms:
$$\text{Sum}_Y = y + 2y + (3y-6)$$

Combine the like terms:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum}_Y &= (1y + 2y + 3y) - 6 \\ &= 6y - 6 \end{aligned}$$

Divide by $n=3$ and simplify the expression:
$$\bar{Y} = \frac{6y-6}{3} = \frac{6y}{3} - \frac{6}{3} = 2y - 2$$

steps.title: State the final answers
steps.content:
The means for sets $X$ and $Y$ in terms of $x$ and $y$ are:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{5x+5}{4}$$ 

$$\bar{Y} = 2y-2$$

parts.content: State what happens to the mean and variance if all the values increase by a constant $k$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the effect on the mean: The mean will also increase by $k$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the effect on the variance: The variance stays the same <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the transformation
steps.content:
The question asks for the effect on the statistics when "all the values increase by a constant $k$". In statistics, adding a constant to every data point is known as a linear transformation (specifically a translation). 

We need to apply the standard rules for how adding a constant affects central tendency and spread.

highlights.text: all the values increase by a constant k

steps.title: Effect on the Mean
steps.content:
The mean is a measure of central tendency (the "center" of the data). If you add a constant $k$ to every single data point, the entire set of data shifts upwards by $k$. Consequently, the center (mean) also shifts by the same amount.

Therefore, the new mean is the old mean plus $k$: 

$$ \text{New Mean} = \text{Old Mean} + k $$

steps.title: Effect on the Variance
steps.content:
The variance is a measure of dispersion or spread (how far the data points are from each other). If we add the same constant $k$ to every value, the data points shift together, but the *distance* between them remains exactly the same.

Since the spread of the data has not changed, the variance remains unchanged:

$$ \text{New Variance} = \text{Old Variance} $$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the properties of linear transformations:

- The **mean** will increase by $k$.
- The **variance** will stay the same.

steps.title: Define the transformation
steps.content:
Let the original data values be $x_1, x_2, 	ext{...}, x_n$. The mean is given by $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$.

When all values increase by a constant $k$, the new values $x_i'$ are:
$$x_i' = x_i + k$$

highlights.text: all the values increase by a constant k

steps.title: Derive the new mean algebraically
steps.content:
We calculate the new mean $\bar{x}'$ by summing the new values and dividing by $n$:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{x}' &= \frac{\sum (x_i + k)}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i + \sum k}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i + nk}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum x_i}{n} + \frac{nk}{n} \\ &= \bar{x} + k \end{aligned}$$

This shows the new mean is the original mean plus $k$.

steps.title: Derive the new variance algebraically
steps.content:
The variance is the average of the squared differences from the mean: $$\text{Var} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}$$.

For the new data, we substitute $x_i' = x_i + k$ and $\bar{x}' = \bar{x} + k$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}' &= \frac{\sum (x_i' - \bar{x}')^2}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum ((x_i + k) - (\bar{x} + k))^2}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum (x_i + k - \bar{x} - k)^2}{n} \\ &= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \\ &= \text{Var} \end{aligned}$$

The constant $k$ cancels out, so the variance remains unchanged.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the algebraic derivation:
1. The mean increases by $k$.
2. The variance stays the same.

parts.content: Given the means of $X$ and $Y$ are equal and that $x$ and $y$ are also equal, find the actual values of the means.
parts.markscheme:
- Equate the means and substitute $y=x$: $\frac{5x+5}{4}=2x-2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $x$: $5x+5=8x-8 \implies 13=3x \implies x=\frac{13}{3}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute $x$ into simplified mean expression: $\bar{Y} = 2\left(\frac{13}{3}\right) - 2 = \frac{26}{3} - \frac{6}{3} = \frac{20}{3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Find expression for mean of X
steps.content:
To find the mean of data set $X$, we sum the elements and divide by the number of terms ($n=4$). This concept is covered in **SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode**.

Sum of $X = (x-4) + x + 2x + (x+9) = 5x + 5$

$$\bar{X} = \frac{5x+5}{4}$$

steps.title: Find expression for mean of Y
steps.content:
Similarly, we find the mean for set $Y$, which has 3 terms.

Sum of $Y = y + 2y + (3y-6) = 6y - 6$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{6y-6}{3}$$ 

We can simplify this expression by dividing both terms in the numerator by 3:

$$\bar{Y} = 2y - 2$$

steps.title: Equate means and solve for x
steps.content:
The question states that the means are equal and that $x=y$. We equate our two expressions and substitute $x$ for $y$ to solve for $x$.

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{X} &= \bar{Y} \\ \dfrac{5x+5}{4} &= 2x - 2 \\ 5x+5 &= 4(2x-2) \\ 5x+5 &= 8x - 8 \\ 13 &= 3x \\ x &= \frac{13}{3} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Use the numeric solver to check the solution for x.

desmos.instructions: Graph y=(5x+5)/4 and y=2x-2 to find the intersection point x-coordinate.

steps.title: Calculate the actual mean value
steps.content:
Now that we have $x$, substitute it back into either mean expression to find the actual value. The expression for $\bar{Y}$ is simpler.

$$\bar{Y} = 2\left(\frac{13}{3}\right) - 2$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{26}{3} - \frac{6}{3} = \frac{20}{3}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the final value to ensure accuracy.

steps.title: Find expression for Mean of X
steps.content:
First, we find an expression for the mean of set $X$ by summing the terms and dividing by the number of terms ($n=4$).

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{X} &= \frac{(x-4) + x + 2x + (x+9)}{4} \\ &= \frac{5x + 5}{4} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find expression for Mean of Y
steps.content:
Next, we find the mean of set $Y$ ($n=3$). We are given that $y=x$, so we substitute $x$ for $y$ immediately.

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{Y} &= \frac{y + 2y + (3y-6)}{3} \\ &= \frac{6y - 6}{3} \\ &= 2y - 2 \\ &= 2x - 2 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: x and y are also equal

steps.title: Set up GDC functions
steps.content:
Since the question states the means are equal, we are looking for the value of the mean where these two expressions intersect. We treat each mean expression as a function of $x$ to graph on the Graphical Display Calculator (GDC).

Let:
$$Y_1 = \frac{5x+5}{4}$$
$$Y_2 = 2x - 2$$

highlights.text: means of X and Y are equal

steps.title: Find intersection using GDC
steps.content: Graph both functions and find their intersection point. The $y$-coordinate of the intersection represents the value of the means.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph mode
2. Input f1(x) = (5x + 5) / 4
3. Input f2(x) = 2x - 2
4. Draw the graph
5. Use G-Solve/Analysis -> Intersection to find the point where lines meet

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = (5X + 5) / 4
3. Enter Y2 = 2X - 2
4. Press [GRAPH] (Adjust [WINDOW] if lines are not visible, e.g., Xmin=0, Xmax=10)
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 5: intersect
6. Press [ENTER] three times to select the curves and guess

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = (5x + 5) / 4
2. Type y = 2x - 2
3. Click the point where the lines cross to see coordinates

calculator.description: Find the intersection of the two mean functions

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives the intersection point $(4.333…, 6.666…)$.

The question asks for the actual values of the means. Since the means are equal, we look at the $y$-coordinate of the intersection.

$$\text{Mean} = 6.67 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{20}{3}$$

questions.specification:
A grouped frequency distribution of the ages of participants in a survey is given below:

| Age Group | Frequency |
|-----------|-----------|
| 10-19     | 5         |
| 20-29     | 12        |
| 30-39     | 18        |
| 40-49     | 10        |
| 50-59     | 5         |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 3: Given a frequency table, calculating the mean, median, modal class and range of the data
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Given a frequency table, calculating the mean, median, modal class and range of the data
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts use a grouped frequency table to compute summary statistics: mean from class midpoints, identify the median class (median age group), and find quartiles/IQR for grouped data.

parts.content: Calculate the mean age of the participants.
parts.markscheme:
- Use midpoints: $14.5, 24.5, 34.5, 44.5, 54.5$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Multiply each midpoint by its frequency:
  $$(14.5 \times 5) + (24.5 \times 12) + (34.5 \times 18) + (44.5 \times 10) + (54.5 \times 5)$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Divide by total frequency (50):
  $$\frac{72.5 + 294 + 621 + 445 + 272.5}{50}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\text{Mean} = 34.1$ years <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<examTip>Remember to use class midpoints rather than class boundaries when calculating the mean of grouped data</examTip>

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the mean of grouped data, we use the formula:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} $$

where $f_i$ is the frequency and $x_i$ is the midpoint of each class interval.

steps.title: Calculate class midpoints
steps.content:
First, we find the midpoint ($x$) for each age group by averaging the lower and upper bounds of the interval:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{10+19}{2} &= 14.5 \\ \frac{20+29}{2} &= 24.5 \\ \frac{30+39}{2} &= 34.5 \\ \frac{40+49}{2} &= 44.5 \\ \frac{50+59}{2} &= 54.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Multiply frequency by midpoint
steps.content:
Next, we multiply each midpoint ($x$) by its frequency ($f$) to find $fx$:

$$\begin{aligned} 14.5 \times 5 &= 72.5 \\ 24.5 \times 12 &= 294.0 \\ 34.5 \times 18 &= 621.0 \\ 44.5 \times 10 &= 445.0 \\ 54.5 \times 5 &= 272.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Sum the values
steps.content:
Now we calculate the total frequency ($\sum f$) and the sum of the products ($\sum fx$):

$$\begin{aligned} \sum f &= 5 + 12 + 18 + 10 + 5 = 50 \\ \sum fx &= 72.5 + 294 + 621 + 445 + 272.5 = 1705 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Finally, divide the sum of the products by the total frequency to find the mean:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Mean} &= \frac{1705}{50} \\ &= 34.1 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Divide total product by total frequency

steps.title: Calculate class midpoints
steps.content:
To calculate the mean of grouped data on a GDC, you first need a single numerical value to represent each group. Calculate the midpoint for each age group by averaging the upper and lower bounds:

$$\text{Midpoint} = \frac{\text{Lower Bound} + \text{Upper Bound}}{2}$$

- $10-19$: $\frac{10+19}{2} = 14.5$
- $20-29$: $\frac{20+29}{2} = 24.5$
- $30-39$: $\frac{30+39}{2} = 34.5$
- $40-49$: $\frac{40+49}{2} = 44.5$
- $50-59$: $\frac{50+59}{2} = 54.5$

steps.title: Enter data into lists
steps.content: Enter the calculated midpoints and the given frequencies into your calculator's list statistics editor. Typically, you place midpoints in List 1 ($L_1$) and frequencies in List 2 ($L_2$).

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Statistics menu (often [MENU] > [2] or [STAT]).
2. In List 1, enter the midpoints: 14.5, 24.5, 34.5, 44.5, 54.5.
3. In List 2, enter the frequencies: 5, 12, 18, 10, 5.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] > [1:Edit].
2. Clear L1 and L2 if necessary.
3. In L1, enter the midpoints: 14.5, 24.5, 34.5, 44.5, 54.5.
4. In L2, enter the frequencies: 5, 12, 18, 10, 5.

desmos.instructions:
Create two lists for the data:
1. Type M = [14.5, 24.5, 34.5, 44.5, 54.5]
2. Type F = [5, 12, 18, 10, 5]

calculator.description: Enter the midpoints and frequencies into lists.

steps.title: Perform 1-Var Stats calculation
steps.content: Use the **1-Variable Statistics** feature to calculate the mean. It is crucial to specify that your data is in the midpoints list and your frequencies are in the frequency list.

gdc.instructions:
1. Select [CALC] (usually F2).
2. Select [SET] (usually F6) to configure settings.
3. Ensure '1Var XList' is set to List 1.
4. Ensure '1Var Freq' is set to List 2.
5. Press [EXIT] and select [1-VAR] (usually F1).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [1:1-Var Stats].
2. Set 'List' to L1 ([2nd] [1]).
3. Set 'FreqList' to L2 ([2nd] [2]).
4. Select [Calculate] and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
Calculate the weighted mean manually since Desmos lacks a direct grouped mean function:
Type: total(M * F) / total(F)

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator displays the mean, usually denoted as $\bar{x}$.

$$\bar{x} = 34.1$$

The mean age is $34.1$ years.

parts.content: Determine the median age group.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify median position: $\frac{n+1}{2} = \frac{50+1}{2} = 25.5$th value <marks>M1</marks>

- Identify correct age group (25.5 lies between cumulative frequency 17 and 35): 30-39 <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Full marks require stating the correct age group.</note>

steps.title: Calculate total frequency
steps.content:
First, we need to find the total number of participants ($n$) by summing the frequencies from the table:

$$n = 5 + 12 + 18 + 10 + 5 = 50$$

steps.title: Find median position
steps.content:
To find the position of the median in an ordered dataset, we use the formula $\frac{n+1}{2}$.

$$\text{Median Position} = \frac{50+1}{2} = 25.5$$

This tells us the median lies between the 25th and 26th values.

steps.title: Calculate cumulative frequencies
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the running total (cumulative frequency) for each group to see where the 25.5th value falls:

*   **10-19**: $5$ (contains values 1 to 5)
*   **20-29**: $5 + 12 = 17$ (contains values 6 to 17)
*   **30-39**: $17 + 18 = 35$ (contains values 18 to 35)

We stop here because the cumulative frequency of $35$ exceeds our target position of $25.5$.

steps.title: State the median group
steps.content:
Since the **25.5th** value lies within the range of the cumulative frequency for the third group (which contains values from the 18th to the 35th), the median age group is **30-39**.

According to the markscheme, identifying the position ($25.5$) earns the method mark (**M1**) and stating the correct group earns the answer mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Understand the Working Inwards method
steps.content:
The **Working Inwards** method finds the median by peeling away equal numbers of data points from the top (lowest age) and bottom (highest age) of the distribution until we reach the middle.

Since the median is the central value, removing the lowest $k$ values and the highest $k$ values simultaneously does not change the location of the median.

steps.title: Peel the first layer
steps.content:
First, we look at the outermost groups:
*   **Top (10-19):** Frequency is 5
*   **Bottom (50-59):** Frequency is 5

Since the frequencies are equal ($5 = 5$), we can subtract 5 from both ends. This completely eliminates both the 10-19 and 50-59 groups.

steps.title: Peel the second layer
steps.content:
Now we move inward to the next available groups:
*   **Top (20-29):** Frequency is 12
*   **Bottom (40-49):** Frequency is 10

The smaller frequency is 10. We subtract 10 from both groups to peel away this layer.

*   **Bottom (40-49):** $10 - 10 = 0$ (This group is now eliminated)
*   **Top (20-29):** $12 - 10 = 2$ (This group still has 2 values remaining)

steps.title: Identify the median group
steps.content:
We are left with 2 values in the **20-29** group (from the top) and the entire middle group of **30-39** (which acts as the new bottom).

We subtract the remaining 2 from the top against the middle group:
*   **Top (20-29):** Subtract 2 $\rightarrow$ 0 remaining (eliminated)
*   **Middle (30-39):** Subtract 2 $\rightarrow$ 16 remaining

Since the only frequencies remaining are in the **30-39** group, the median must lie here.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The median age group is **30-39**.

parts.content: Calculate the interquartile range (IQR) of the ages.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify first quartile using GDC/midpoints: $Q_1 = 24.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify third quartile using GDC/midpoints: $Q_3 = 44.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate IQR: $Q_3 - Q_1 = 44.5 - 24.5 = 20$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct final answer with working shown. Accept IQR calculated via linear interpolation: approx 16.8 (range 16.2 - 16.9).</note>

<examTip>Remember to identify which age groups contain Q1 and Q3 before calculating their values</examTip>

steps.title: Calculate class midpoints
steps.content:
To calculate the interquartile range using a GDC, we first need to represent each age group by a single value. We use the midpoint of each interval.

Calculate the midpoint for each group by averaging the lower and upper bounds:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{10–19: } & \frac{10+19}{2} = 14.5 \\ \text{20–29: } & \frac{20+29}{2} = 24.5 \\ \text{30–39: } & \frac{30+39}{2} = 34.5 \\ \text{40–49: } & \frac{40+49}{2} = 44.5 \\ \text{50–59: } & \frac{50+59}{2} = 54.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Input data into GDC
steps.content: Enter the midpoints and frequencies into your graphing calculator to calculate the 1-variable statistics.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Statistics menu.
2. Enter midpoints in List 1 and frequencies in List 2.
3. Select Calculation > One-Variable.
4. Set X List to List 1 and Frequency List to List 2.
5. Calculate.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] > Edit.
2. Enter the midpoints (14.5, 24.5, 34.5, 44.5, 54.5) into list L1.
3. Enter the frequencies (5, 12, 18, 10, 5) into list L2.
4. Press [STAT] > CALC > 1:1-Var Stats.
5. Set List: L1 and FreqList: L2.
6. Calculate.

desmos.instructions: Desmos does not have a direct 1-Var Stats frequency list function in the standard calculator, but you can create a list of data by repeating values or use the formula: median(data) and quartiles.

calculator.description: Calculate 1-Variable Statistics using midpoints and frequencies

steps.title: Identify quartiles
steps.content:
From the calculator output, identify the values for the first quartile ($Q_1$) and the third quartile ($Q_3$).

$$Q_1 = 24.5$$
$$Q_3 = 44.5$$

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference between the third and first quartiles.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{IQR} &= Q_3 - Q_1 \\ &= 44.5 - 24.5 \\ &= 20 \end{aligned}$$

The interquartile range is 20.

gdc.instructions: Compute the difference between Q3 and Q1.

ti84.instructions: Compute the difference between Q3 and Q1 found in the previous step.

steps.title: Construct cumulative frequency table
steps.content:
To calculate quartiles using linear interpolation, we first need the cumulative frequencies. This helps us locate the position of each quartile within the data.

Adding the frequencies step by step:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{10-19: } & 5 \\ \text{20-29: } & 5 + 12 = 17 \\ \text{30-39: } & 17 + 18 = 35 \\ \text{40-49: } & 35 + 10 = 45 \\ \text{50-59: } & 45 + 5 = 50 \end{aligned}$$

The total frequency is $$N = 50$$.

steps.title: Calculate the First Quartile (Q1)
steps.content:
The position of $$Q_1$$ is at $$\frac{N}{4}$$. 

$$\text{Position} = \frac{50}{4} = 12.5$$

Looking at the cumulative frequencies, the 12.5th value falls in the **20-29** group (which contains values from the 6th to the 17th). 

We use the linear interpolation formula:
$$Q_1 = L + \frac{P - F_{prev}}{f_{quartile}} \times w$$

Where:
- $$L = 19.5$$ (Lower boundary of the group)
- $$P = 12.5$$ (Position of the quartile)
- $$F_{prev} = 5$$ (Cumulative frequency before this group)
- $$f_{quartile} = 12$$ (Frequency of this group)
- $$w = 10$$ (Class width, e.g., $$29.5 - 19.5$$)

Substituting the values:

$$Q_1 = 19.5 + \frac{12.5 - 5}{12} \times 10$$

$$Q_1 = 19.5 + \frac{7.5}{12} \times 10 = 19.5 + 6.25 = 25.75$$

steps.title: Calculate the Third Quartile (Q3)
steps.content:
The position of $$Q_3$$ is at $$\frac{3N}{4}$$.

$$\text{Position} = \frac{3 \times 50}{4} = 37.5$$

Looking at the cumulative frequencies, the 37.5th value falls in the **40-49** group (which contains values from the 36th to the 45th).

Using the same interpolation formula:
- $$L = 39.5$$ (Lower boundary)
- $$P = 37.5$$ (Position)
- $$F_{prev} = 35$$ (Cumulative frequency before this group)
- $$f_{quartile} = 10$$ (Frequency of this group)
- $$w = 10$$ (Class width)

Substituting the values:

$$Q_3 = 39.5 + \frac{37.5 - 35}{10} \times 10$$

$$Q_3 = 39.5 + \frac{2.5}{10} \times 10 = 39.5 + 2.5 = 42.0$$

steps.title: Calculate the Interquartile Range (IQR)
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the IQR by finding the difference between the third and first quartiles.

$$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$$

$$IQR = 42.0 - 25.75 = 16.25$$

This value lies within the accepted range (16.2 - 16.9) specified in the markscheme note.

questions.specification:
The times in minutes for seven students to complete a logic puzzle were measured. The results are shown below.

$25, 30, 28, 120, 22, 35, 20$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 5: Given a data set, finding the value of the standard deviation, variance and IQR
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 5: Given a data set, finding the value of the standard deviation, variance and IQR
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks to compute mean and standard deviation from an ungrouped data set, which fits the summary-statistics/standard deviation leaf topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 1: Given some values for data, finding each: mean, mode, median and range
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Given some values for data, finding each: mean, mode, median and range
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) involve selecting/justifying an appropriate measure of central tendency (mean/median/mode) for a data set with an outlier; this aligns with finding/using mean, median, and mode for a given data set.

parts.content: Find the mean and standard deviation of these times.
parts.markscheme:
Data: $25, 30, 28, 120, 22, 35, 20$

Number of data values ($N$) $= 7$

**For the Mean ($\bar{x}$):**
- Sum of times ($\sum x$) $= 25+30+28+120+22+35+20 = 280$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Mean ($\bar{x}$) $= \frac{280}{7} = 40$ <marks>A1</marks>

**For the Standard Deviation ($	\sigma_x$):**
- $\sum x^2 = 25^2+30^2+28^2+120^2+22^2+35^2+20^2$
  $= 625+900+784+14400+484+1225+400 = 18818$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Variance ($	\sigma_x^2$) $= \frac{\sum x^2}{N} - \bar{x}^2 = \frac{18818}{7} - 40^2$
  $= 2688.28\dots - 1600 = 1088.28\dots$
- Standard deviation ($	\sigma_x$) $= \sqrt{1088.28\dots} \approx 33.0$ (3 s.f.) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: State which of the mean, median or mode you consider would be most appropriate to use as a measure of central tendency to represent the data in this case.
parts.markscheme: The median <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: For each of the two measures of average you did not choose in Part 2, give a reason why you consider it inappropriate.
parts.markscheme:
Data sorted: $20, 22, 25, 28, 30, 35, 120$

- The mean is inappropriate because it is significantly affected by the outlier ($120$), making it not representative of the typical times <marks>R1</marks>
- The mode is inappropriate because there are no repeated values in the data set, so a mode does not exist <marks>R1</marks>

questions.specification:
A set of $N$ observations, $x$, is coded using $y = x - 45$. It is found that $\sum y = 100$ and $\sum y^2 = 1000$.
The standard deviation of $x$ is $5$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 7: Determining the effect on mean, mode, median, range, variance, standard deviation and IQR given specific transformations of data
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 7: Determining the effect on mean, mode, median, range, variance, standard deviation and IQR given specific transformations of data
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses coding/translation y=x−45 and given Σy, Σy² to determine N, mean and standard deviation after adding an observation—i.e., effects of data transformations and updating mean/variance/SD.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Data given as parameters and finding the value of the data given some relationship/values of the central tendancy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Finds unknowns (N and an added data value) from relationships involving summary statistics (mean and standard deviation) and provided sums.

parts.content: Find the number of observations, $N$.
parts.markscheme:
Let $y = x - 45$.
We are given $\sum y = 100$, $\sum y^2 = 1000$, and $\sigma_x = 5$.
Since standard deviation is unaffected by change of origin, $\sigma_y = \sigma_x = 5 \implies \sigma_y^2 = 25$.

- Express the mean of $y$: $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y}{N} = \frac{100}{N}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Use the variance formula for $y$: $\sigma_y^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - (\bar{y})^2$
- Substitute values: $25 = \frac{1000}{N} - (\frac{100}{N})^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Multiply by $N^2$: $25N^2 = 1000N - 10000$
- Simplify: $25N^2 - 1000N + 10000 = 0 \implies N^2 - 40N + 400 = 0$
- Solve for $N$: $(N - 20)^2 = 0$
- State the number of observations: $N = 20$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Use invariance of standard deviation
steps.content:
First, we determine the standard deviation of the coded data $y$. The coding $y = x - 45$ subtracts a constant from every observation. 

Standard deviation is a measure of spread, so it is **invariant** (does not change) when a constant is added or subtracted. Therefore, the standard deviation of $y$ is equal to the standard deviation of $x$.

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_x = 5$$

steps.title: Set up the variance equation
steps.content:
We use the computational formula for variance: $\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - (\bar{y})^2$. 

Since $\sigma_y = 5$, the variance is $\sigma_y^2 = 25$. We also know the mean is $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y}{N}$. Substituting these and the given sums into the formula:

$$25 = \frac{1000}{N} - \left( \frac{100}{N} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Solve the equation for N
steps.content:
Now we solve for $N$. First, square the term in the parentheses:

$$25 = \frac{1000}{N} - \frac{10000}{N^2}$$

To eliminate the fractions, multiply the entire equation by $N^2$:

$$25N^2 = 1000N - 10000$$

Bring all terms to one side to form a quadratic equation:

$$25N^2 - 1000N + 10000 = 0$$

calculator.description: Verify the calculation by substituting N=20 back into the variance expression

steps.title: Find the roots using a calculator
steps.content:
You can solve the quadratic equation $25N^2 - 1000N + 10000 = 0$ using the polynomial solver on your calculator or by graphing.

Alternatively, divide the equation by $25$ to simplify it manually:

$$N^2 - 40N + 400 = 0$$

Factor the perfect square:

$$(N - 20)^2 = 0$$

Therefore, $N = 20$.

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation menu > Polynomial > Degree 2. Enter a=25, b=-1000, c=10000. Press SOLVE.

ti84.instructions: Press APPS, select PLYSMLT2, choose ROOT FINDER. Set Order to 2. Enter coefficients: a=25, b=-1000, c=10000. Press SOLVE.

desmos.instructions: Type 25x^2 - 1000x + 10000 = 0 to see the vertical line at the solution.

calculator.description: Solve the quadratic equation for N

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The number of observations is:

$$N = 20$$

steps.title: Reverse the coding
steps.content:
The question uses a coded variable $y$ to represent the data. To use the **Reverting to Original Variables** approach, we first express $x$ in terms of $y$. 

Given the coding $y = x - 45$, we can rearrange this to:
$$x = y + 45$$

steps.title: Express sum of x
steps.content:
Now we find an expression for the sum of the original data, $\sum x$, in terms of $N$.

Using summation properties:
$$\sum x = \sum (y + 45) = \sum y + \sum 45$$

Since we are summing the constant 45 exactly $N$ times (once for each observation), $\sum 45 = 45N$. We substitute the given value $\sum y = 100$:
$$\sum x = 100 + 45N$$

steps.title: Express sum of x squared
steps.content:
Next, we derive an expression for $\sum x^2$ in terms of $N$. First, square the expression for $x$:
$$x^2 = (y + 45)^2 = y^2 + 90y + 2025$$

Now sum over all observations:
$$\sum x^2 = \sum y^2 + 90\sum y + \sum 2025$$

Substitute the given values $\sum y^2 = 1000$ and $\sum y = 100$, and note that $\sum 2025 = 2025N$:
$$\begin{aligned} \sum x^2 &= 1000 + 90(100) + 2025N \\ &= 1000 + 9000 + 2025N \\ &= 10000 + 2025N \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply variance formula
steps.content:
The question states the standard deviation of $x$ is 5, so the variance is $\sigma^2 = 5^2 = 25$. We use the standard variance formula:

$$\text{Variance} = \frac{\sum x^2}{N} - \left(\frac{\sum x}{N}\right)^2$$

Substitute our expressions for $\sum x^2$ and $\sum x$:
$$25 = \frac{10000 + 2025N}{N} - \left(\frac{100 + 45N}{N}\right)^2$$

steps.title: Simplify the equation
steps.content:
To solve for $N$, we simplify the terms. It helps to split the fractions first:

$$25 = \left(\frac{10000}{N} + 2025\right) - \left(\frac{100}{N} + 45\right)^2$$

Expand the squared term $\left(\frac{100}{N} + 45\right)^2 = \frac{10000}{N^2} + \frac{9000}{N} + 2025$:

$$\begin{aligned} 25 &= \frac{10000}{N} + 2025 - \left(\frac{10000}{N^2} + \frac{9000}{N} + 2025\right) \\ 25 &= \frac{10000}{N} + 2025 - \frac{10000}{N^2} - \frac{9000}{N} - 2025 \\ 25 &= \frac{1000}{N} - \frac{10000}{N^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for N
steps.content:
Multiply the entire equation by $N^2$ to remove the denominators:

$$25N^2 = 1000N - 10000$$

Rearrange into a standard quadratic form ($ax^2 + bx + c = 0$):
$$25N^2 - 1000N + 10000 = 0$$

Divide by 25 to simplify:
$$N^2 - 40N + 400 = 0$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation > Polynomial > Degree 2. Enter 1, -40, 400.

ti84.instructions: Press APPS, select PLYSMLT2, then ROOT FINDER. Enter coefficients a=1, b=-40, c=400.

desmos.instructions: Type x^2 - 40x + 400 = 0 and find the x-intercept.

calculator.description: Solve the quadratic equation for N

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Factor the perfect square:
$$(N - 20)^2 = 0$$

Therefore:
$$N = 20$$

parts.content:
An additional observation, $x_{N+1}$, is added to the set. The new mean of the $N+1$ observations is $50.5$.

Find the value of $x_{N+1}$.
parts.markscheme:
Initial mean of $x$ is $\bar{x} = \bar{y} + 45$.

- Calculate the mean of $y$: $\bar{y} = \frac{100}{20} = 5$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the initial sum of $x$: $\bar{x} = 5 + 45 = 50 \implies \sum x = 20 \times 50 = 1000$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the new sum of $x$: $\sum x' = (N+1) \times 50.5 = 21 \times 50.5 = 1060.5$
- Calculate the value of the additional observation: $x_{N+1} = 1060.5 - 1000 = 60.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up variance equation
steps.content:
First, we need to find the number of observations, $N$. We are given that the standard deviation of $x$ is 5. Since adding or subtracting a constant does not affect the spread of data, the standard deviation of $y$ is the same as $x$.

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_x = 5$$

Using the variance formula from the data booklet:

steps.title: Solve for N
steps.content:
Substituting the given values $\sum y = 100$ and $\sum y^2 = 1000$ into the variance formula $\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - (\frac{\sum y}{N})^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} 5^2 &= \frac{1000}{N} - \left(\frac{100}{N}\right)^2 \\ 25 &= \frac{1000}{N} - \frac{10000}{N^2} \end{aligned}$$

Multiply by $N^2$ to solve the quadratic:

$$\begin{aligned} 25N^2 &= 1000N - 10000 \\ N^2 - 40N + 400 &= 0 \\ (N - 20)^2 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

So, $N = 20$.

ti84.instructions: Calculate 1000/20 - (100/20)^2 to see if it equals 25

calculator.description: Verify the solution for N by substituting back into the variance expression

steps.title: Calculate initial sum of x
steps.content:
Now we un-code the sum to find the initial sum of the original values $x$. Since $y = x - 45$, we have $x = y + 45$. 

Summing over all $N=20$ observations:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum x &= \sum (y + 45) \\ \sum x &= \sum y + 20(45) \\ \sum x &= 100 + 900 \\ \sum x &= 1000 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find new total sum
steps.content:
We are told that adding one observation $x_{N+1}$ changes the mean to $50.5$. The new number of observations is $N + 1 = 21$.

Using the mean formula $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{n}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum x_{\text{new}} &= (N+1) \times \bar{x}_{\text{new}} \\ &= 21 \times 50.5 \\ &= 1060.5 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: new mean of the $N+1$ observations is $50.5$

steps.title: Calculate additional observation
steps.content:
The value of the additional observation is the difference between the new sum and the initial sum.

$$\begin{aligned} x_{N+1} &= \sum x_{\text{new}} - \sum x \\ &= 1060.5 - 1000 \\ &= 60.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Strategy: Working with Coded Values
steps.content:
We will solve this using the **Coded Values** approach as requested. 

Instead of un-coding everything immediately, we will:
1. Use the statistical properties of the coded variable $y$ to find the number of observations $N$.
2. Calculate the new sum of $y$ including the additional observation.
3. Find the additional coded value $y_{N+1}$ and then convert it back to $x_{N+1}$.

This method is often computationally simpler because the numbers are smaller.

steps.title: Setup variance equation
steps.content:
First, we need to find $N$. We are given the standard deviation of $x$ is $5$. Adding or subtracting a constant does not change the standard deviation, so the standard deviation of $y$ is also $\sigma_y = 5$, and the variance is $\sigma_y^2 = 25$.

We use the computational formula for variance (from **Topic 4: Statistics**):

$$ \sigma^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - \left(\frac{\sum y}{N}\right)^2 $$

Substituting the given values $\sum y = 100$ and $\sum y^2 = 1000$:

$$ 25 = \frac{1000}{N} - \left(\frac{100}{N}\right)^2 $$

steps.title: Solve for N
steps.content:
Now we solve this equation for $N$.

$$\begin{aligned} 25 &= \frac{1000}{N} - \frac{10000}{N^2} \\ 25N^2 &= 1000N - 10000 \\ 25N^2 - 1000N + 10000 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Divide the entire equation by 25 to simplify:

$$\begin{aligned} N^2 - 40N + 400 &= 0 \\ (N - 20)^2 &= 0 \\ N &= 20 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the quadratic equation into the solver or graph y = x^2 - 40x + 400

desmos.instructions: Type x^2 - 40x + 400 = 0 to see the intercept

steps.title: Find new coded mean
steps.content:
We are given the new mean of the $N+1$ observations for $x$ is $50.5$. We need to convert this into a mean for the coded variable $y$ using the coding formula $y = x - 45$.

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{x}_{new} &= 50.5 \\ \bar{y}_{new} &= \bar{x}_{new} - 45 \\ \bar{y}_{new} &= 50.5 - 45 \\ \bar{y}_{new} &= 5.5 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: new mean of the $N+1$ observations is $50.5$

steps.title: Calculate new coded sum
steps.content:
Now we calculate the sum of the coded values for the new set of $N+1$ observations. Since $N=20$, the new set has $21$ observations.

$$ \text{Sum}_{new} = (N+1) \times \bar{y}_{new} $$

$$\begin{aligned} \sum y_{new} &= 21 \times 5.5 \\ \sum y_{new} &= 115.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find additional coded observation
steps.content:
The sum of the original observations was $\sum y = 100$. The difference between the new sum and the old sum represents the value of the additional coded observation $y_{N+1}$.

$$\begin{aligned} y_{N+1} &= \sum y_{new} - \sum y_{old} \\ y_{N+1} &= 115.5 - 100 \\ y_{N+1} &= 15.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Un-code to find result
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the coded observation $y_{N+1}$ back to the original variable $x_{N+1}$ using the rearrangement of the coding formula $x = y + 45$.

$$\begin{aligned} x_{N+1} &= y_{N+1} + 45 \\ x_{N+1} &= 15.5 + 45 \\ x_{N+1} &= 60.5 \end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $60.5$

parts.content: Calculate the standard deviation of all $N+1$ observations.
parts.markscheme:
Find the original sum of squares $\sum x^2$:
- Use the formula $\sigma_x^2 = \frac{\sum x^2}{N} - \bar{x}^2$ to express $\sum x^2$: $\sum x^2 = N(\sigma_x^2 + \bar{x}^2)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute values: $\sum x^2 = 20(5^2 + 50^2) = 20(2525) = 50500$ <marks>A1</marks>

Find the new sum of squares $\sum (x')^2$:
- Add the new observation squared: $\sum (x')^2 = 50500 + 60.5^2$
- Calculate the total: $\sum (x')^2 = 50500 + 3660.25 = 54160.25$ <marks>M1</marks>

Calculate the new standard deviation $\sigma_x'$:
- Use the variance formula: $\sigma_x'^2 = \frac{54160.25}{21} - 50.5^2$
- Substitute values: $\sigma_x'^2 \approx 2579.06 - 2550.25 \approx 28.81$
- Calculate the result: $\sigma_x' = \sqrt{28.8095\dots} \approx 5.37$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Find the number of observations N
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the value of $N$. We are given the standard deviation of $x$ is $5$, and since adding or subtracting a constant does not change the standard deviation, the standard deviation of $y$ is also $5$. Thus, the variance is $\sigma^2 = 25$.

Using the variance formula for $y$:
$$\sigma_y^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - \left(\frac{\sum y}{N}\right)^2$$

Substituting the given values:
$$25 = \frac{1000}{N} - \left(\frac{100}{N}\right)^2$$

Multiplying by $N^2$ and solving the resulting quadratic $N^2 - 40N + 400 = 0$ gives:
$$N = 20$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Equation Solver to find N.

ti84.instructions: Use the numeric solver or graph y = 1000/x - (100/x)^2 - 25 to find the root.

desmos.instructions: Graph the equation to find the x-intercept.

calculator.description: Solve for N using the variance relation

steps.title: Calculate original mean and sum of squares
steps.content:
We need to work with the original variable $x$. First, find the mean of $x$. Since $y = x - 45$, we have $x = y + 45$. 

The mean of $y$ is $\bar{y} = \frac{100}{20} = 5$. Therefore, the mean of $x$ is:
$$\bar{x} = 5 + 45 = 50$$

Next, use the variance formula for $x$ to find $\sum x^2$. Rearranging $\sigma_x^2 = \frac{\sum x^2}{N} - \bar{x}^2$ gives:
$$\sum x^2 = N(\sigma_x^2 + \bar{x}^2)$$

Substitute $N=20$, $\sigma_x=5$, and $\bar{x}=50$:
$$\sum x^2 = 20(5^2 + 50^2) = 20(25 + 2500) = 50500$$

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of squares for x

steps.title: Update the sum of squares with new observation
steps.content:
Now we incorporate the new observation. Based on the context of the problem, the new observation is $x_{new} = 60.5$.

Add the square of this new value to our sum of squares:
$$\sum (x')^2 = \sum x^2 + (60.5)^2$$
$$\sum (x')^2 = 50500 + 3660.25 = 54160.25$$

calculator.description: Calculate the new sum of squares

steps.title: Calculate the new standard deviation
steps.content:
First, find the new mean $\bar{x}'$. The original sum of $x$ was $N\bar{x} = 20(50) = 1000$. Adding the new observation gives the new sum $1000 + 60.5 = 1060.5$. The new sample size is $21$.

$$\bar{x}' = \frac{1060.5}{21} = 50.5$$

Now, apply the variance formula for the new set of data:
$$\sigma_{new}^2 = \frac{\sum (x')^2}{N+1} - (\bar{x}')^2$$

Substitute our values:
$$\sigma_{new}^2 = \frac{54160.25}{21} - 50.5^2$$

Using a calculator:
$$\sigma_{new} = \sqrt{\frac{54160.25}{21} - 2550.25} \approx 5.37$$

ti84.instructions: Compute the standard deviation using the variance formula.

steps.title: Find the number of observations N
steps.content:
First, we need to find the number of original observations, $N$. We are given that the standard deviation of $x$ is $5$. Since the standard deviation is invariant under translation (shifting data by a constant), the standard deviation of the coded variable $y$ is the same as $x$.

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_x = 5$$

Using the variance formula from the statistics section of the data booklet, $\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - \mu^2$, we can set up an equation for $N$:

steps.title: Solve for N
steps.content:
Substitute the given values $\sum y = 100$, $\sum y^2 = 1000$, and $\sigma_y^2 = 5^2 = 25$ into the variance formula:

$$\begin{aligned} 25 &= \frac{1000}{N} - \left(\frac{100}{N}\right)^2 \\ 25 &= \frac{1000}{N} - \frac{10000}{N^2} \\ 25N^2 &= 1000N - 10000 \\ N^2 - 40N + 400 &= 0 \\ (N - 20)^2 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $N = 20$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the variance using N=20 to confirm it equals 25

ti84.instructions: Calculate the variance using N=20 to confirm it equals 25

desmos.instructions: Calculate the variance using N=20 to confirm it equals 25

steps.title: Code the new observation
steps.content:
We are adding a new observation. Based on the working shown in the markscheme, the new observation is $x_{new} = 60.5$. To continue using the coded variable method, we convert this to $y$ using the coding formula $y = x - 45$.

$$y_{new} = 60.5 - 45 = 15.5$$

steps.title: Update the sums of y
steps.content:
Now we update the sums for the coded variable $y$ by adding the new value. The new number of observations is $N' = 20 + 1 = 21$.

New sum of $y$:
$$\sum y_{new} = 100 + 15.5 = 115.5$$

New sum of squared $y$:
$$\sum y_{new}^2 = 1000 + (15.5)^2 = 1000 + 240.25 = 1240.25$$

gdc.instructions: Compute the new sum of y squared

ti84.instructions: Compute the new sum of y squared

desmos.instructions: Compute the new sum of y squared

calculator.description: Calculate the new sum of squares

steps.title: Calculate the new mean of y
steps.content:
Calculate the new mean of the coded variable, $\bar{y}'$:

$$\bar{y}' = \frac{\sum y_{new}}{N'} = \frac{115.5}{21} = 5.5$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the new sum by the new N

ti84.instructions: Divide the new sum by the new N

desmos.instructions: Divide the new sum by the new N

steps.title: Calculate the new standard deviation
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the new standard deviation for $y$. Since standard deviation is invariant under translation ($y = x - 45$), the standard deviation of $x$ will be the same as that of $y$.

Using the variance formula $\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - \bar{y}^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{y}'^2 &= \frac{1240.25}{21} - (5.5)^2 \\ &\approx 59.0595 - 30.25 \\ &\approx 28.81 \end{aligned}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\sigma_{x}' = \sigma_{y}' = \sqrt{28.8095\dots} \approx 5.37$$

questions.specification:
The daily number of visitors to a small art gallery, chosen at random during a certain month, was found to be:

$$120, 150, 130, 140, 160, 125, 135, 145, 1000, 120, 140$$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.3—Mean, median, mode. Mean of grouped data, standard deviation. Quartiles, IQR > Question Type 1: Given some values for data, finding each: mean, mode, median and range
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Given some values for data, finding each: mean, mode, median and range
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The task is to choose and compute an appropriate measure of central tendency (mean/median/mode) for a given ungrouped data set and justify why others are unsuitable (outlier 1000 affects mean; mode not informative). This aligns with calculating and interpreting mean/median/mode from raw data.

parts.content: Choose and calculate an appropriate measure of central tendency (mean, mode or median) to summarise these visitor numbers. Explain briefly why the other measures are not suitable.
parts.markscheme:
- Organize the data in ascending order: $120, 120, 125, 130, 135, 140, 140, 145, 150, 160, 1000$ <marks>M1</marks>

**Mean**
- Sum of values $= 120+150+130+140+160+125+135+145+1000+120+140 = 2365$
- Number of values $= 11$
- Mean $= \frac{2365}{11} = 215$

**Median**
- Position $= \frac{11+1}{2} = 6^{\text{th}}$ value
- Median $= 140$

**Mode**
- Values $120$ and $140$ both appear $2$ times. The data is bimodal.

- State the appropriate measure: The appropriate measure of central tendency is the median <marks>A1</marks>

- State the value: The median $= 140$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Reason for mean unsuitability: The mean is not suitable because it is significantly affected by the outlier ($1000$), making it not representative of the typical daily visitors <marks>R1</marks>

- Reason for mode unsuitability: The mode is not suitable because the data is bimodal ($120$ and $140$), so there isn't a single clear mode to represent the central tendency <marks>R1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Sort the data manually
steps.content:
To analyze the data for central tendency, we first organize the list of visitor numbers in ascending order. This allows us to easily find the median and spot patterns.

Original data:

$$120, 150, 130, 140, 160, 125, 135, 145, 1000, 120, 140$$

Sorted data:

$$120, 120, 125, 130, 135, 140, 140, 145, 150, 160, 1000$$

steps.title: Find the median position
steps.content:
The median is the middle value of the ordered dataset. Since there are $n=11$ values, the median is at the $\left( \frac{n+1}{2} \right)^{\text{th}}$ position.

$$\text{Position} = \frac{11+1}{2} = 6^{\text{th}}\text{ value}$$

steps.title: Identify the median value
steps.content:
Counting to the 6th value in our sorted list:

$$120, 120, 125, 130, 135, \mathbf{140}, 140, 145, 150, 160, 1000$$

The median is $140$. This represents the middle of the distribution.

steps.title: Inspect for outlier and mean
steps.content:
By inspecting the sorted list, we can see a significant outlier: $1000$. This value is much larger than the other values (which range from $120$ to $160$).

If we calculate the mean:

$$\text{Mean} = \frac{2365}{11} = 215$$

The mean ($215$) is much higher than the majority of the days, so it is skewed by the outlier and not representative.

gdc.instructions: Sum the values and divide by 11:

ti84.instructions: Enter the data into a list (optional) or sum the values directly:

calculator.description: Calculate the mean to verify it is skewed

steps.title: Inspect for mode
steps.content:
We look for the most frequent values. In the sorted list:
- $120$ appears twice
- $140$ appears twice

The data is **bimodal**. Because there are two modes, a single mode cannot accurately summarize the "center" of the data.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on our inspection, the **median** is the most appropriate measure.

- **Appropriate Measure:** Median = $140$
- **Reason against Mean:** It is significantly affected by the outlier ($1000$).
- **Reason against Mode:** The data is bimodal ($120$ and $140$), so there is no unique central value.

steps.title: Input data into GDC
steps.content:
First, enter the list of visitor numbers into your graphing display calculator (GDC). This is usually done in the Statistics or List menu.

Data: $120, 150, 130, 140, 160, 125, 135, 145, 1000, 120, 140$

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [1:Edit]. Enter the values into L1.

calculator.description: Enter the dataset into a list.

steps.title: Calculate 1-Var Stats
steps.content:
Use the **1-Var Stats** (One-Variable Statistics) function on your calculator to compute the summary statistics for the list. Retrieve the mean ($\bar{x}$) and the median ($Med$) directly from the output.

From the GDC output:
- Mean: $$\bar{x} = 215$$
- Median: $$Med = 140$$

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [1:1-Var Stats]. Ensure List is L1, then Calculate. Read x̄ (mean) and Med (median).

steps.title: Identify the mode
steps.content:
To find the mode, you can sort the list on your GDC or inspect the frequency of values. 

Looking at the data, the values $120$ and $140$ each appear twice, while all other values appear once. Since there are two most frequent values, the data is **bimodal**.

gdc.instructions: Use the Sort Ascending tool on List 1 to group identical values together.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [2:SortA(]. Enter L1, close parenthesis, and press [ENTER]. Go back to [STAT] > [1:Edit] to view sorted data.

desmos.instructions: Type sort(L) to see the ordered list.

calculator.description: Sort the data to find the mode.

steps.title: Select the appropriate measure
steps.content:
Compare the calculated statistics to the dataset. The majority of the daily visitor numbers are between $120$ and $160$. The value $1000$ is significantly larger than the rest, making it an **outlier**.

The **median** is the most appropriate measure because it is not influenced by this extreme outlier and represents the typical number of visitors ($140$).

Final Answer: **Median = 140**

steps.title: Explain why mean is unsuitable
steps.content: The mean is not suitable because it is sensitive to extreme values. The outlier ($1000$) pulls the mean up to $215$, which is higher than almost all the actual daily visitor counts (except the outlier itself). Therefore, it does not accurately represent the center of the data.

steps.title: Explain why mode is unsuitable
steps.content: The mode is not suitable because the dataset is **bimodal** (having two modes: $120$ and $140$). A single measure of central tendency should ideally provide one unique value to summarize the data, but here the mode is ambiguous.

parts.content: Find the values of $\sum(x-8)$ and $\sum(x-8)^2$.
parts.markscheme:
Number of seeds ($N$) $= 15$.

Given $\sum x^2 = 1575$ and mean mass $\bar{x} = 10$ grams.

- **Calculate** $\sum x$:
  $\sum x = N \bar{x} = 15 \times 10 = 150$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Calculate** $\sum(x-8)$:
  Using the property $\sum(x-c) = \sum x - Nc$:
  $\sum(x-8) = \sum x - (15 \times 8) = 150 - 120 = 30$ <marks>M1</marks>

**Method 1**: Using expansion

- **Substitute** values into the expansion formula $\sum(x-c)^2 = \sum x^2 - 2c\sum x + Nc^2$:
  $\sum(x-8)^2 = 1575 - 2(8)(150) + 15(8^2)$ <marks>M1</marks>
  $\sum(x-8)^2 = 1575 - 2400 + 960$

- **State** final answer:
  $\sum(x-8)^2 = 135$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Method 2**: Using variance

- **Calculate** variance of $x$:
  $\sigma_x^2 = \frac{\sum x^2}{N} - \bar{x}^2 = \frac{1575}{15} - (10)^2 = 105 - 100 = 5$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Variance of coded data is the same: $\sigma_{(x-8)}^2 = 5$.
  Mean of coded data: $\overline{x-8} = \bar{x} - 8 = 10 - 8 = 2$.
  Using $\sigma_{(x-8)}^2 = \frac{\sum(x-8)^2}{N} - (\overline{x-8})^2$:
  $5 = \frac{\sum(x-8)^2}{15} - (2)^2 \Rightarrow 9 = \frac{\sum(x-8)^2}{15}$

- **State** final answer:
  $\sum(x-8)^2 = 135$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the values provided in the question specification:

*   Number of seeds, $N = 15$
*   Sum of squares, $\sum x^2 = 1575$
*   Mean mass, $\bar{x} = 10$

steps.title: Calculate sum of x
steps.content:
To expand the expressions, we first need to find $\sum x$. We can find this using the definition of the mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{N} \implies \sum x = N \times \bar{x}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\sum x = 15 \times 10 = 150$$

steps.title: Find sum of (x - 8)
steps.content:
Now we can solve the first part of the question. We apply the sigma notation rule $\sum(x - c) = \sum x - \sum c$. Remember that summing a constant $N$ times is equal to $N \times c$.

$$\begin{aligned} \sum(x-8) &= \sum x - \sum 8 \\ &= \sum x - (15 \times 8) \\ &= 150 - 120 \\ &= 30 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Expand the squared expression
steps.content:
For the second part, $\sum(x-8)^2$, we start by algebraically expanding the term inside the summation:

$$(x-8)^2 = x^2 - 16x + 64$$

steps.title: Apply summation rules
steps.content:
Now we apply the summation $\sum$ to each term in our expansion. The sigma operator is linear, meaning we can distribute it:

$$\sum(x-8)^2 = \sum(x^2 - 16x + 64)$$

Distributing the sigma:

$$\sum(x-8)^2 = \sum x^2 - 16\sum x + \sum 64$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the known values into our expanded equation. Recall that $\sum 64$ means adding the constant 64 exactly $N=15$ times.

$$\begin{aligned} \sum(x-8)^2 &= 1575 - 16(150) + (15 \times 64) \\ &= 1575 - 2400 + 960 \\ &= 135 \end{aligned}$$

**Answer:**
$$\begin{aligned} \sum(x-8) &= 30 \\ \sum(x-8)^2 &= 135 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
We are given the following statistics for the seeds:
- Number of seeds: $$N = 15$$
- Sum of squares: $$\sum x^2 = 1575$$
- Mean mass: $$\bar{x} = 10$$

steps.title: Calculate sum of (x-8)
steps.content:
First, we find $$\sum(x-8)$$. We can distribute the summation or use the property that subtracting a constant from every data point reduces the sum by $$N \times \text{constant}$$.

Using basic summation rules:
$$\begin{aligned} \sum(x-8) &= \sum x - \sum 8 \\ &= N\bar{x} - (15 \times 8) \\ &= (15 \times 10) - 120 \\ &= 150 - 120 \\ &= 30 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the transformed data

steps.title: Calculate variance of original data
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the variance of the original data ($$\sigma_x^2$$) using the computational formula for variance. This formula is standard in **Topic 4: Statistics**.

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_x^2 &= \frac{\sum x^2}{N} - (\bar{x})^2 \\ &= \frac{1575}{15} - 10^2 \\ &= 105 - 100 \\ &= 5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply variance properties
steps.content:
We use the property that **variance is invariant under translation**. Adding or subtracting a constant from a data set does not change its spread (variance).

Therefore, the variance of the new data $$(x-8)$$ is equal to the variance of the original data $$x$$:
$$\sigma_{x-8}^2 = \sigma_x^2 = 5$$

steps.title: Calculate new mean
steps.content:
The mean is affected by translation. If we subtract 8 from every data point, the new mean is simply the old mean minus 8.

$$\overline{x-8} = \bar{x} - 8 = 10 - 8 = 2$$

steps.title: Solve for sum of squares
steps.content:
Now we use the variance formula for the new data set $$(x-8)$$ to find $$\sum(x-8)^2$$.

$$\sigma_{x-8}^2 = \frac{\sum(x-8)^2}{N} - (\overline{x-8})^2$$

Substituting our known values:
$$\begin{aligned} 5 &= \frac{\sum(x-8)^2}{15} - 2^2 \\ 5 &= \frac{\sum(x-8)^2}{15} - 4 \\ 9 &= \frac{\sum(x-8)^2}{15} \\ \sum(x-8)^2 &= 9 \times 15 \\ \sum(x-8)^2 &= 135 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final sum of squares

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The required values are:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum(x-8) &= 30 \\ \sum(x-8)^2 &= 135 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Recall the definition of mean
steps.content:
To calculate the mean (often denoted as $\bar{x}$), we use the direct formula which requires summing all the data values and dividing by the total number of values:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{n}$$

steps.title: Sum the data values
steps.content:

steps.content:
Adding these values together gives:

$$\sum x = 36$$

steps.title: Divide by the count
steps.content:
There are $10$ values in the data set ($n = 10$). We divide the sum by this count to find the mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{36}{10}$$

Performing the division:

$$\bar{x} = 3.6$$

parts.content: Determine the median of the data set.
parts.markscheme:
- Arrange the data in ascending order: $1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6$

- Identify the middle values: Since $n = 10$ (even), the median is the average of the 5th and 6th values

- Calculate the median: $\frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<examTip>When finding the median with even number of values, remember to take the average of the middle two numbers</examTip>

steps.title: Arrange data in ascending order
steps.content:
To find the median using the manual ordering method, we first list the data points from smallest to largest.

The original set is:
$$x = \{1, 4, 5, 6, 4, 6, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

Arranged in ascending order:
$$1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6$$

steps.title: Identify the middle values
steps.content:
Count the number of data points, $n$. Here, there are 10 values ($n=10$).

Since $n$ is an even number, the median is the average of the two middle values found at positions $\frac{n}{2}$ and $\frac{n}{2} + 1$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Position 1: } & \frac{10}{2} = \text{5th value} \\ \text{Position 2: } & 5 + 1 = \text{6th value} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Locate values in ordered list
steps.content:
Looking at our ordered list, we identify the 5th and 6th numbers:

$$1, 1, 2, 3, \mathbf{4}, \mathbf{4}, 4, 5, 6, 6$$

Both the 5th and 6th values are $4$.

steps.title: Calculate the median
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the average of these two middle values:

$$\text{Median} = \frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4$$

calculator.description: Calculate the average of the two middle values

parts.content: Find the lower and upper quartiles of the data set.

- Identify the first half of the data $\{1, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and find the median:
  $Q_1 = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the second half of the data $\{4, 4, 5, 6, 6\}$ and find the median:
  $Q_3 = 5$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Sort the data set
steps.content:
First, we arrange the data set in ascending order to locate the positions of the quartiles. There are 10 values in the original set.

Original: $$\{1, 4, 5, 6, 4, 6, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

Sorted: $$\{1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6\}$$

steps.title: Divide into halves
steps.content:
Since there is an even number of data points ($n=10$), we split the data exactly down the middle into two equal halves of 5 values each.

$$\underbrace{\{1, 1, 2, 3, 4\}}_{\text{Lower Half}} \quad \mid \quad \underbrace{\{4, 4, 5, 6, 6\}}_{\text{Upper Half}}$$ 

This concept relates to **SL 4.3** in the syllabus involving Quartiles.

steps.title: Find the Lower Quartile (Q1)
steps.content:
The lower quartile ($Q_1$) is the median of the **lower half** of the data. The lower half has 5 values, so the median is the middle (3rd) term.

Lower Half: $$\{1, 1, \mathbf{2}, 3, 4\}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_1 = 2$$

highlights.text:
Identify the first half of the data {1, 1, 2, 3, 4} and find the median:
  Q_1 = 2

steps.title: Find the Upper Quartile (Q3)
steps.content:
The upper quartile ($Q_3$) is the median of the **upper half** of the data. The upper half also has 5 values, so the median is the middle (3rd) term of this subset.

Upper Half: $$\{4, 4, \mathbf{5}, 6, 6\}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_3 = 5$$

highlights.text:
Identify the second half of the data {4, 4, 5, 6, 6} and find the median:
  Q_3 = 5

Scroll down the results screen to find the values for the first quartile ($Q_1$) and the third quartile ($Q_3$).

The calculator display will show:
- $Q_1 = 2$
- $Q_3 = 5$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The lower quartile is 2 and the upper quartile is 5.

$$\begin{aligned} Q_1 &= 2 \\ Q_3 &= 5 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Identify any outliers in the data set using the interquartile range (IQR).
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the interquartile range: $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 5 - 2 = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the outlier boundaries: Lower bound $= Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 2 - (1.5 \times 3) = -2.5$ and Upper bound $= Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 5 + (1.5 \times 3) = 9.5$

- Conclude there are no outliers since all data points lie between $-2.5$ and $9.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks only if both bounds are calculated and a clear conclusion is stated</note>

steps.title: Sort the data
steps.content:
First, we arrange the data set in ascending order to find the quartiles more easily. There are 10 data points.

$$1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6$$

steps.title: Find the quartiles
steps.content:
Since there are 10 values ($n=10$), we split the data into a lower half and an upper half of 5 values each.

Lower half: $1, 1, \mathbf{2}, 3, 4$ 
Upper half: $4, 4, \mathbf{5}, 6, 6$

The lower quartile ($Q_1$) is the median of the lower half, and the upper quartile ($Q_3$) is the median of the upper half.

$$\begin{aligned} Q_1 &= 2 \\ Q_3 &= 5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference between the upper and lower quartiles.

$$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 5 - 2 = 3$$

steps.title: Calculate outlier boundaries
steps.content:
Outliers are values that fall below the lower boundary or above the upper boundary. The standard formula uses $1.5 \times IQR$.

Lower boundary:
$$Q_1 - 1.5 \times IQR = 2 - 1.5(3) = 2 - 4.5 = -2.5$$

Upper boundary:
$$Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR = 5 + 1.5(3) = 5 + 4.5 = 9.5$$

steps.title: Identify outliers
steps.content:
We check if any values in our data set lie outside the interval $[-2.5, 9.5]$.

- Minimum value: $1$ (inside)
- Maximum value: $6$ (inside)

Since all data values are between $-2.5$ and $9.5$, there are **no outliers**.

steps.title: Enter data into GDC
steps.content:
First, enter the given data set into your graphing display calculator (GDC) to perform a statistical analysis.

Data set: $$x = \{1, 4, 5, 6, 4, 6, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the Statistics / List menu and enter the data into a new list.

calculator.description: Enter the data into a list.

steps.title: Find quartiles using GDC
steps.content:
Use the 1-Variable Statistics function on your calculator to find the first quartile ($Q_1$) and the third quartile ($Q_3$).

From the GDC output:

$$\begin{aligned} Q_1 &= 2 \\ Q_3 &= 5 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Select the calculation option for 1-variable statistics for your list. Look for Q1 (lower quartile) and Q3 (upper quartile).

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [1:1-Var Stats]. Select L1 as the List. Scroll down to find Q1 and Q3.

steps.title: Calculate the IQR
steps.content:
The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference between the third and first quartiles. This step helps define the spread of the middle 50% of the data.

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 5 - 2 = 3$$

steps.title: Determine outlier boundaries
steps.content:
Calculate the boundaries for outliers using the standard formula: $1.5 \times \text{IQR}$ below $Q_1$ and above $Q_3$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Lower Bound} &= Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR} \\ &= 2 - (1.5 \times 3) \\ &= 2 - 4.5 \\ &= -2.5 \\ \\ \text{Upper Bound} &= Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR} \\ &= 5 + (1.5 \times 3) \\ &= 5 + 4.5 \\ &= 9.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify outliers
steps.content:
Compare the original data values to these boundaries. An outlier would be any value less than $-2.5$ or greater than $9.5$.

The data range is from $1$ to $6$. Since all data values lie within the interval $[-2.5, 9.5]$, there are no outliers.

**Conclusion:** There are no outliers.

questions.specification:
A summary of the heights, $h$ cm, of $30$ plants gave the following information:
$\sum(h - 15) = 120$ and $\sum(h - 15)^2 = 850$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > AHL 4.14—Properties of discrete and continuous random variables > Question Type 8: For given values of either summary statistic, finding another corresponding value using properties
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 8: For given values of either summary statistic, finding another corresponding value using properties
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses given summary statistics in shifted form (∑(h−15) and ∑(h−15)^2) to compute variance and then derive ∑h^2; this is finding unknown summary measures from other summary measures using statistical identities.

parts.content: Find the variance of the heights and hence find the value of $\sum h^2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify parameters and coded variable: $N = 30$, let $y = h - 15$.

- Calculate mean of coded data: $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y}{N} = \frac{120}{30} = 4$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate variance of coded data using $\sigma_y^2 = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - (\bar{y})^2$: $\sigma_y^2 = \frac{850}{30} - 4^2 = \frac{37}{3}$ $(12.33\dots)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State variance of heights: $\sigma_h^2 = \frac{37}{3} \approx 12.3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate mean of heights: $\bar{h} = \bar{y} + 15 = 19$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute into variance formula rearranged as $\sum h^2 = N(\sigma_h^2 + \bar{h}^2)$: $\sum h^2 = 30\left(\frac{37}{3} + 19^2\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final value: $\sum h^2 = 11200$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Define the coded variable
steps.content:
To simplify the calculation, we identify the coded variable $y$ based on the given expressions. The question provides sums in terms of $(h - 15)$, so we define $y = h - 15$. We also note that the number of plants $N$ is 30.

We are given:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum y &= 120 \\ \sum y^2 &= 850 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the mean of y
steps.content:
First, we calculate the mean of the coded variable $y$ using the standard formula $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y}{N}$.

$$\bar{y} = \frac{120}{30} = 4$$

steps.title: Calculate the variance of y
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the variance of $y$ using the formula $\text{Var}(y) = \frac{\sum y^2}{N} - (\bar{y})^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_y^2 &= \frac{850}{30} - 4^2 \\ &= \frac{85}{3} - 16 \\ &= \frac{85 - 48}{3} \\ &= \frac{37}{3} \approx 12.33\dots \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate sum of squares divided by N minus mean squared

ti84.instructions: Calculate sum of squares divided by N minus mean squared

desmos.instructions: Calculate sum of squares divided by N minus mean squared

steps.title: State the variance of heights
steps.content:
A key property of variance is that adding or subtracting a constant from every data point does not change the spread of the data. Therefore, the variance of the original heights $h$ is equal to the variance of the coded variable $y$.

$$\text{Var}(h) = \text{Var}(y) = \frac{37}{3} \approx 12.3$$

steps.title: Find the mean of heights
steps.content:
To find $\sum h^2$, we will need the mean of the original heights $\bar{h}$. Since $y = h - 15$, we have $h = y + 15$. The mean transforms linearly:

$$\bar{h} = \bar{y} + 15 = 4 + 15 = 19$$

steps.title: Formula for sum of squares
steps.content:
We rearrange the variance formula $\sigma_h^2 = 	\frac{\sum h^2}{N} - (\bar{h})^2$ to solve for $\sum h^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\sum h^2}{N} &= \sigma_h^2 + (\bar{h})^2 \\ \sum h^2 &= N(\sigma_h^2 + (\bar{h})^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Substitute the known values ($N=30$, $\sigma_h^2 = \frac{37}{3}$, and $\bar{h}=19$) into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \sum h^2 &= 30\left(\frac{37}{3} + 19^2\right) \\ &= 30\left(\frac{37}{3} + 361\right) \\ &= 30\left(\frac{37}{3}\right) + 30(361) \\ &= 370 + 10830 \\ &= 11200 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify summation rules
steps.content:
To use the Algebraic Expansion method, we need two key properties of sigma notation (summation). For a constant $c$ and variable $x$, recall that:

1.  **Sum of a difference:** $\sum (x - c) = \sum x - \sum c$
2.  **Sum of a constant:** $\sum_{i=1}^{N} c = N \times c$

We are given $N=30$ plants, so summing a constant will multiply it by $30$.

steps.title: Solve for sum of heights
steps.content:
First, we expand the linear equation $\sum(h - 15) = 120$ to find $\sum h$.

$$\begin{aligned} \sum(h - 15) &= 120 \\ \sum h - \sum 15 &= 120 \\ \sum h - (30 \times 15) &= 120 \\ \sum h - 450 &= 120 \\ \sum h &= 570 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the constant term and add to 120

ti84.instructions: Calculate the constant term and add to 120

desmos.instructions: Calculate the constant term and add to 120

steps.title: Expand quadratic summation
steps.content:
Next, we expand the quadratic equation $\sum(h - 15)^2 = 850$. We start by expanding the term inside the summation algebraically: $(h-15)^2 = h^2 - 30h + 225$.

$$\begin{aligned} \sum(h^2 - 30h + 225) &= 850 \\ \sum h^2 - 30\sum h + \sum 225 &= 850 \\ \sum h^2 - 30\sum h + (30 \times 225) &= 850 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for sum of squares
steps.content:
Now we substitute the value of $\sum h = 570$ we found in Step 1 into our expanded equation to solve for $\sum h^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} \sum h^2 - 30(570) + 6750 &= 850 \\ \sum h^2 - 17100 + 6750 &= 850 \\ \sum h^2 - 10350 &= 850 \\ \sum h^2 &= 11200 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Add the terms to the other side

ti84.instructions: Add the terms to the other side

desmos.instructions: Add the terms to the other side

calculator.description: Solve for sum of h squared

steps.title: Calculate the variance
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the variance of the heights using the standard formula $\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum h^2}{N} - (\bar{h})^2$. 

First, find the mean $\bar{h}$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\sum h}{N} = \frac{570}{30} = 19$$

Now calculate the variance:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma^2 &= \frac{11200}{30} - 19^2 \\ &= \frac{1120}{3} - 361 \\ &= \frac{1120}{3} - \frac{1083}{3} \\ &= \frac{37}{3} \approx 12.3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The variance of the heights is approximately **12.3** ($37/3$) and the value of $\sum h^2$ is **11200**.

Note: The question asked for variance first, then $\sum h^2$. This algebraic expansion method finds them in the reverse order, which is perfectly valid.

questions.specification:
40 students took a standardized test to see where they landed on a scale from 0 to 100. The frequency distribution of their scores is given below.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/555b2b7a-ffda-4ae4-956b-b9ff35f915bd)

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a), (c), and (d) use a grouped frequency table to compute and interpret the mean, and to solve for unknown changes in class frequencies given a new mean—core skills in mean/summary statistics from a frequency table.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.2—Histograms, CF graphs, box plots > Question Type 1: Constructing histograms given the frequency distribution table
